mate: To equal; to be equal to. For thus the mastful chestnut mates the skies.
To enervate; to subdue; to crush. Audacity doth almost bind and mate the weaker sort of minds.
In chess, the state of the king so situated that he cannot escape.
mateless: Having no mate or companion. Materia Medica, a general name for every substance used in medicine.
An auxiliary branch of the science of medicine, which treats of the nature and properties of all the substances that are employed for the cure of diseases.
material: Consisting of matter; not spiritual; as material substance; material bodies.
The substance or matter of which any thing is made; as, wool is the material of cloth; rags are the material of paper.
mpair: To be lessened or worn out.
In crystallography, when a different number of faces is presented by the prism, and by each summit; but the three numbers follow no law of progression.
mab: In northern mythology, the queen of the imaginary beings called fairies.
mac: in names of Scotch and Irish origin, signifies son.
macaroni: A kind of biscuit made of flour, eggs, sugar and almonds, and dressed with butter and spices.
A sort of droll or fool, and hence, a fop; a fribble; a finical fellow.
macaronic: Pertaining to or like a macaroni; empty; trifling; vain; affected.
Consisting of a mixture or jumble of ill formed or ill connected words.
A kind of burlesque poetry, in which native words are made to end in Latin terminations, or Latin words are modernized.
macauco: A name of several species of quadrupeds of the genus Lemur.
macaw: The name of a race of beautiful fowls of the parrot kind, under the genus Psittacus.
macao: The name of a race of beautiful fowls of the parrot kind, under the genus Psittacus.
maccabees: The name of two apocryphal books in the Bible.
mace: An ensign of authority borne before magistrates. Originally, the mace was a club or instrument of war, made of iron and much used by cavalry. It was in the shape of a coffee mill. Being no longer a weapon of war, its form is changed; it is made of silver or copper gilt, and ornamented with a crown, globe and cross. A leaden mace, A heavy iron mace.

A spice; the second coat which covers the nutmeg, a thin and membranaceous substance of an oleaginous nature and yellowish color, being in flakes divided into many ramifications; it is extremely fragrant and aromatic.
mace-bearer: A person who carries a mace before men in authority.
macerate: To make lean; to wear away.
To mortify; to harass with corporeal hardships; to cause to pine or waste away. Out of excessive zeal they macerate their bodies and impair their health.
To steep almost to solution; to soften and separate the parts of a substance by steeping it in a fluid, or by the digestive process. So we Say, food is macerated in the stomach.
macerated: Made thin or lean; steeped almost to solution.
macerating: Making lean; steeping almost to solution; softening.
maceration: The act or the process of making thin or lean by wearing away, or by mortification.
The act, process or operation of softening and almost dissolving by steeping in a fluid. The saliva serves for the maceration and dissolution of the meat into chyle.
mace-reed: A plant of the genus Typha.
machiavelian: Pertaining to Machiavel, or denoting his principles; politically cunning; crafty; cunning in political management.
One who adopts the principles of Machiavel.
machiavelism: The principles of Machiavel, or practice in conformity to them; political cunning and artifice, intended to favor arbitrary power.
machicolation: In old castles, the pouring of hot substances through apertures in the upper part of the gate assailants; or the apertures themselves.
machinate: To plan; to contrive; to form a scheme.
machination: The act of planning or contriving a scheme for executing some purpose, particularly an evil purpose; an artful design formed with deliberation.
machinator: One that forms a scheme, or who plots with evil designs.
machine: An artificial work, simple or complicated, that serves to apply or regulate moving power, or to produce motion, so as to save time or force. The simple machines are the six mechanical powers; the lever, the pulley, the axis and wheel, the wedge, the screw, and the inclined plane. Complicated machines are such as combine two or more of these powers for the production of motion or force.
An engine; an instrument of force. With inward arms the dire machine they load.
Supernatural agency in a poem, or a superhuman being introduced into a poem to perform some exploit.
machinery: A complicated work, or combination of mechanical powers in a work, designed to increase, regulate or apply motion and force; as the machinery of a watch or other chronometer.
Machines in general. The machinery of a cotton-mill is often moved by a single wheel.

In epic and dramatic poetry, superhuman beings introduced by the poet to solve difficulty, or perform some exploit which exceeds human power; or the word may signify the agency of such beings, as supposed deities, angels, demons and the like. Nee Deus intersit, nisi dignus vindice nodus Incidit/‘ . A deity is not to be introduced, unless a difficulty occurs that requires the intervention of a god. The machinery of Milton’s Paradise Lost, consists of numerous superhuman personages. Pope’s Rape of the Lock is rendered very interesting by the machinery of sylphs.
machining: Denoting the machinery of a poem.
machinist: A constructor of machines and engines, or one well versed in the principles of machines.
macigno: A species of stone of two varieties, one of a grayish yellow color, the other of a bluish gray color.
mackerel: A species of fish of the genus Scomber, an excellent table fish .
A pander or pimp. Mackerel-gale, in Dryden, may mean a gate that ripples the surface of the sea, or one which is suitable for catching mackerel, as this fish is caught with the bait in motion.
mackerel-sky: A sky streaked or marked like a mackerel.
macle: A name given to chiastolite or hollow spar.
maclurite: A mineral of a brilliant pale green color, so called in honor of Maclure, the mineralogist.
macrocosm: The great world; the universe, or the visible system of worlds; opposed to microcosm, or the world of man.
macrology: Long and tedious talk; prolonged discourse without matter; superfluity of words.
mactation: The act of killing a victim for sacrifice.
macula: A spot, as on the skin, or on the surface of the sun or other luminous orb.
maculation: The act of spotting a spot; a stain.
mad: Disordered in intellect; distracted; furious. We must bind our passions in chains, lest like mad folks, they break their locks and bolts.
Proceeding from disordered intellect or expressing it; as a mad demeanor.
Enraged; furious; as a mad bull. And being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them, even to strange cities. Acis 26:77.

Inflamed to excess with desire; excited with violent and unreasonable passion or appetite; infatuated; followed properly by after. The world is running made after farce, the extremity of bad poetry. “Mad upon their idols,” would be better rendered, “Mad after their idols.” Jeremiah 50:38.
Distracted with anxiety or trouble; extremely perplexed. Thou shalt be mad for the sight of thine eyes-- Deuteronomy 28:34.
madam: An appellation or complimentary title given to married and elderly ladies, or chiefly to them.
madapple: A plant of the genus Solanum.
mad-cap: A violent, rash, hot-headed person; a madman.
madden: To become mad; to act as if mad. They rave, recite and madden round the land.
madder: A plant of the genus Rubia, one species of which is much used in dyeing red. The root is used in medicine as an aperient and detergent, and is in great reputation as an emmenagogue. It is cultivated in France and Holland.
madefy: To make wet or moist; to moisten.
madeira: A rich wine made on the isle of Madeira.
mademoiselle: A young woman, or the title given to one; miss; also, the puppet sent from the French metropolis to exhibit the prevailing fashions. 758)
madhouse: A house where insane persons are confined for cure or for restraint.
madly: Without reason or understanding; rashly; wildly.
With extreme folly or infatuated zeal or passion. ADLY.
madman: A man raving or furious with disordered intellect; a distracted man.
One inflamed with extravagant passion, and acting contrary to reason.
madness: Distraction; a state of disordered reason or intellect, in which the patient raves or is furious. There are degrees of madness as of folly.
Extreme folly; headstrong passion and rashness that act in opposition to reason; as the madness of a mob.
Wildness of passion; fury; rage; as the madness of despair.
madona: A term of compellation equivalent to madam. It is given to the virgin Mary.
madonna: A term of compellation equivalent to madam. It is given to the virgin Mary.
madrepore: A submarine substance of a stony hardness, resembling coral. It consists of carbonate of lime with some animal matter. It is of a white color, wrinkled on the surface, and full of cavities or cells, inhabited by a small animal. From a liquor discharged by this animal, the substance is said to be formed. Madrepores constitute a genus of polypiers, of variable forms, always garnished with radiated plates.
madreporite: A name given to certain petrified bones found in Normandy, in France, belonging to a cetaceous fish or to a species of crocodile. These bones contain many little brown lines in zigzag, resembling entangled threads. They have none of the properties of madrepore.
A variety of limestone, so called on account of its occurring in radiated prismatic concretions resembling the stars of madrepores. When rubbed, it emits the smell of sulphurated hydrogen gas.
madrier: A thick plank armed with iron plates, with a cavity to receive the mouth of a petard, with which it is applied to any thing intended to be broken down; also, a plank used for supporting the earth in mines.
madrigal: A little amorous poem, sometimes called a pastoral poem, containing a certain number of free unequal verses, not confined to the scrupulous regularity of a sonnet or the subtilty of the epigram, but containing some tender and delicate, though simple thought, suitably expressed. RIGAL.
An elaborate vocal composition in five or six parts.
madwort: A plant of the genus Alyssum.
maestoso: an Italian word signifying majestic, a direction in music to play the part with grandeur and strength.
magazine: A store of arms, ammunition or provisions; or the building in which such store is deposited. It is usually a public store or storehouse.
In ships of war, a close room in the hold, where the gunpowder is kept. Large ships have usually two magazines.

A pamphlet periodically published, containing miscellaneous papers or compositions. The first publication of this kind in England, was the Gentleman’s Magazine, which first appeared in 1731, under the name of Sylvanus Urban, by Edward Cave, and which is still continued.
magaziner: One who writes for a magazine.
mage: A magician. Magellanic clouds, whitish clouds, or appearances like clouds near the south pole, which revolve like the stars; so called from Magellan, the navigator. They are three in number.
maggot: A worm or grub; particularly, the flyworm, from the egg of the large blue or green fly. This maggot changes into a fly.
maggoty-headed: Having a head full of whims.
magi: Wise men or philosophers of the East.
magian: Pertaining to the Magi, a sect of philosophers in Persia.

One of the sect of the Persian Magi, who hold that there are two principles, one that cause of good, the other of evil. The knowledge of these philosophers was deemed by the vulgar to be supernatural.
magianism: The philosophy or doctrines of the Magi.
magic: The art or science of putting into action the power of spirits; or the science of producing wonderful effects by the aid of superhuman beings, or of departed spirits; sorcery; enchantment.

The secret operations of natural causes. Natural magic, the application of natural causes to passive subjects, by which surprising effects are produced. Celestial magic, attributes to spirits a kind of dominion over the planets, and to the planets an influence over men. Superstitious or geotic magic, consists in the invocation of devils or demons, and supposes some tacit or express agreement between them and human beings. Magic square, a square figure, formed by a series of numbers in mathematical proportion, so disposed in parallel and equal ranks, as that the sums of each row or line taken perpendicularly, horizontally, or diagonally, are equal. Magic lantern, a dioptric machine invented by Kircher, which, by means of a map in a dark room, exhibits images of objects in their distinct colors and proportions, with the appearance of life itself.
Pertaining to magic; used in magic; as a magic wand; magic art.
Performed by magic, the agency of spirits, or by the invisible powers of nature; as magical effects.
magical: Pertaining to magic; used in magic; as a magic wand; magic art.
Performed by magic, the agency of spirits, or by the invisible powers of nature; as magical effects.
magically: By the arts of magic; according to the rules or rites of magic; by enchantment.
magician: One skilled in magic; one that practices the black art; an enchanter; a necromancer; a sorcerer or sorceress.
magisterial: Pertaining to a master; such as suits a master; authoritative.
Proud; lofty; arrogant; imperious; domineering. Pretenses go a great way with men that take fair words and magisterial looks for current payment.
In chimistry, pertaining to magistery, which see.
magisterially: With the air of a master; arrogantly; authoritatively.
magisterialness: The air and manner of a master; haughtiness; imperiousness; peremptoriness.
magistery: Among chimists, a precipitate; a fine substance deposited by precipitation; usually applied to particular kinds of precipitate, as that of bismuth, coal, crab’s eyes, sulphur
magistracy: The office or dignity of a magistrate. Duelling is not only an usurpation of the divine prerogative, but it is an insult upon magistracy.
magistrate: A public civil officer, invested with the executive government of some branch of it. In this sense, a king is the highest or first magistrate, as is the President of the United States. But the word is more particularly applied to subordinate officers, as governors, intendants, prefects, mayors, justices of the peace, and the like. The magistrate must have his reverence; the laws their authority.
magistratic: Having the authority of a magistrate.
magna charta: The great charter, so called obtained by the English barons from king John, A.D. 1215. This name is also given to the charter granted to the people of England in the ninth year of Henry III. and confirmed by Edward I.
A fundamental constitution which guarantees rights and privileges.
magnanimity: Greatness of mind; that elevation or dignity of soul, which encounters danger and trouble with tranquillity and firmness, which raises the possessor above revenge, and makes him delight in acts of benevolence, which makes him disdain injustice and meanness, and prompts him to sacrifice personal ease, interest and safety for the accomplishment of useful and noble objects.
magnanimous: Great of mind; elevated in soul or in sentiment; brave; disinterested; as a magnanimous prince or general.

Dictated by magnanimity; exhibiting nobleness of soul; liberal and honorable; not selfish. There is an indissoluble union between a magnanimous policy and the solid rewards of public prosperity and felicity.
magnanimously: With greatness of mind; bravely; with dignity and elevation of sentiment.
magnesia: A primitive earth, having for its base a metallic substance, called magnesium. It is generally found in combination with other substances. It is absorbent and antacid, and moderately cathartic.
magnesian: Pertaining to magnesia, or partaking of its qualities.
magnesite: Carbonated magnesia, or magnesia combined with silex. It occurs in amorphous masses, or in masses tuberous and spungiform; its color is yellowish gray, or white with spots, and dendritic delineations of blackish brown.
magnet: The lodestone; an ore of iron which has the peculiar properties of attracting metallic iron, of pointing to the poles, and of dipping or inclining downwards. These properties it communicates to iron by contact. A bar of iron to which these properties are imparted, is called an artificial magnet.
magnetic: Pertaining to the magnet; possessing the properties of the magnet, or corresponding properties; as a magnetic bar of iron, or a magnetic needle.
Attractive. She that had all magnetic force along-- .
magnetical: Pertaining to the magnet; possessing the properties of the magnet, or corresponding properties; as a magnetic bar of iron, or a magnetic needle.
Attractive. She that had all magnetic force along-- .
magnetically: By means of magnetism; by the power of attraction.
magnetics: The science or principles of magnetism.
magnetism: That branch of science which treats of the properties of the magnet, the power of the lodestone

Power of attraction; as the magnetism of interest. Animal magnetism, a sympathy supposed to exist between the magnet and the human body, by means of which the magnet is said to be able to cure diseases; or a fluid supposed to exist throughout nature, and to be the medium of influence between celestial bodies, and the earth and human bodies.
magnetize: To communicate magnetic properties to any thing; as, to magnetize a needle. Seven of Deslon’s patients were magnetized at Dr. Franklin’s house.
To acquire magnetic properties; to become magnetic. A bar of iron standing some time in an inclined position, will magnetize.
magnifiable: That may be magnified; worthy of being magnified or extolled.
magnificence: Grandeur of appearance; greatness and splendor of show or state; as the magnificence of a palace or of a procession; the magnificence of a Roman triumph.
magnificent: Grand in appearance; splendid; pompous. Man he made, and for him built . Magnificent this world.
magnificently: With splendor of appearance, or pomp of show. The minister was magnificently entertained at court.
With exalted sentiments. We can never conceive too magnificently of the Creator and = his works.
magnifier: One who magnifies; one who extols or exalts in praises.
A glass that magnifies; a convex lens which increases the apparent magnitude of bodies.
magnify: To make great or greater; to increase the apparent dimensions of a body. A convex lens magnifies the bulk of a body to the eye.
To make great in representation; to extol; to exalt in description or praise. The embassador magnified the king and queen.

To extol; to exalt; to elevate; to raise in estimation. Thee that day . Thy thunders magnified. The Lord magnified Solomon’ exceedingly.7 Chronicles 29:25. To magnify one’s self, to raise in pride and pretensions. He shall magnify in his heart. Danie! 8:25.
magnifying: Enlarging apparent bulk or dimensions; extolling; exalting.
magniloquence: A lofty manner of speaking; tumid, pompous words or style.
magnitude: Extent of dimensions or parts; bulk; size; applied to things that have length, breadth or thickness.
Greatness; grandeur. With plain heroic magnitude of mind.
Greatness, in reference to influence or effect; importance. In affairs or magnitude, disdain not to take counsel.
magnolia: The laurel-leafed tulip tree, of several species.
magpie: A chattering bird of the genus Corvus.
maguey: 2. A species of aloe in Mexico, which furnished the natives with timber for their buildings. Its leaves were used for covering the roofs of their houses, and for paper, clothing and cordage. The maguey is a species of the Genus Agave, and is now cultivated in Mexico, for the purpose of preparing from its leaves a spirituous liquor called pulque.
mahogany: A tree of the genus Swietenia, growing in the tropical climates of America. The wood is of a reddish or brown color, very hard, and susceptible of a fine polish. Of this are made our most beautiful and durable pieces of cabinet furniture.
mahometan: This word and the name of the Arabian prophet, so called, are written in many different ways. The best authorized and most correct orthography seems to be Mohammed, Mohammedan.
mohammedan: This word and the name of the Arabian prophet, so called, are written in many different ways. The best authorized and most correct orthography seems to be Mohammed, Mohammedan.
A follower of Mohammed, the founder of the religion of Arabia and Persia.
mahound: Formerly a contemptuous name for Mohammed and the devil
maid: An unmarried woman, or a young unmarried woman; a virgin.
It is used in composition, to express the feminine gender, as in maid-servant.
maiden: An unmarried woman, or a young unmarried woman; a virgin.
It is used in composition, to express the feminine gender, as in maid-servant.
A maid; also, an instrument for beheading criminals, and another for washing linen.
Pertaining to a young woman or virgin; as maiden charms.
maidenhair: A plant of the genus Adiantum.
maidenhood: The state of being a maid or virgin; virginity: . The modest lore of maidenhood.
maidenliness: The behavior that becomes a maid; modesty; gentleness.
maidenly: Like a maid; gentle; modest; reserved.
maidmarian: A dance; so called from a buffoon dressed like a man.
mail: A coat of steel net-work, formerly worn for defending the body against swords, poniardsThe mail was of two sorts, chain and plate mail; the former consisting of iron rings, each having four others inserted into it; the latter consisting of a number of small lamins of metal, laid over one another like the scales of a fish, and sewed down to a strong linen or leathern jacket.
Armor; that which defends the body. We strip the lobster of his scarlet mail. We read also of shirts of mail, and gloves of mail.
In ships, a square machine composed of rings interwoven, like net-work, used for rubbing off the loose hemp on lines and white cordage.
A bag for the conveyance of letters and papers, particularly letters conveyed from one post office to another, under public authority.
mail-coach: A coach that conveys the public mails.
mailed: Covered with a mail or with armor; inclosed and directed, as letters in a bundle.
mailing: Investing with a coat of mail; inclosing in a wrapper and directing to a post office.
maim: To deprive of the use of a limb, so as to render a person less able to defend himself in fighting, or to annoy his adversary.
To deprive of a necessary part; to cripple; to disable. You maim’d the jurisdiction of all bishops.
The privation of the use of a limb or member of the body, so as to render the sufferer less able to defend himself or to annoy his adversary.
The privation of any necessary part; a crippling. Surely there is more cause to fear lest the want thereof be a maim, than the use of it a blemish.
maiming: Disabling by depriving of the use of a limb; crippling; rendering lame or defective.
main: Principal; chief; that which has most power in producing an effect, or which is mostly regarded in prospect; as the main branch or tributary stream of a river; the main timbers of an edifice; a main design; a main object. Our main interest is to be as happy as we can, and as long as possible.
Mighty; vast; as the main abyss.
Important; powerful. This young prince, with a train of young noblemen and gentlemen, not with any main army, came over to take possession of his patrimony.
Strength; force; violent effort; as in the phrase, “with might and main.”
The gross; the bulk; the greater part. The main of them may be reduced to language and an improvement in wisdom-- .
main-land: The continent; the principal land, as opposed to an isle.
mainly: Chiefly; principally. He is mainly occupied with domestic concerns.
Greatly; to a great degree; mightily; .
main-mast: The principal mast in a ship or other vessel.
main-keel: The principal keel, as distinguished from the false keel.
mainor: The old law phrase, to be taken as a thief with the mainor, signifies, to be taken in the very act of killing venison or stealing wood, or in preparing so to do; or it denotes the being taken with the thing stolen upon him.
mainpernable: That may be admitted to give surety by mainpernors; that may be mainprized.
mainpernor: In law, a surety for a prisoner's appearance in court at a day. Mainpernors differ from bail, in that a man’s bail may imprison or surrender him before the stipulated day of appearance; mainpernors can do neither; they are bound to produce him to answer all charges whatsoever.

In law, a surety for a prisoner’s appearance in court at a day. Mainpernors differ from bail, in that a man’s bail may imprison or surrender him before the stipulated day of appearance; mainpernors can do neither; they are bound to produce him to answer all charges whatsoever.
mainprize: In law, a writ directed to the sheriff, commanding him to take sureties for the prisoner’s appearance, and to let him go at large. These sureties are called mainpernors.
Deliverance of a prisoner on security for his appearance at a day.
To suffer a prisoner to go at large, on his finding sureties, mainpernors, for his appearance at a day.
main-sail: The principal sail in a ship. The main-sail of a ship or brig is extended by a yard attached to the main-mast, and that of a sloop, by the boom.
main-sheet: The sheet that extends and fastens the main-sail.
mainswear: To swear falsely; to perjure one’s self.
maintain: To hold, preserve or keep in any particular state or condition; to support; to sustain; not to suffer to fail or decline; as, to maintain a certain degree of heat in a furnace; to maintain the digestive process or powers of the stomach; to maintain the fertility of soil; to maintain present character or reputation.
To hold; to keep; not to lose or surrender; as, to maintain a place or post.
To continue; not to suffer to cease; as, to maintain a conversation.
To keep up; to uphold; to support the expense of; as, to maintain state or equipage. What maintains one vice would bring up two children.
To support with food, clothing and other conveniences; as, to maintain a family by trade or labor.
maintainable: That may be maintained, supported, preserved or sustained.
That may be defended or kept by force or resistance; as, a military post is not maintainable.
That may be defended by argument or just claim; vindicable; defensible.
maintained: Kept in any state; preserved; upheld; supported; defended; vindicated.
maintainer: One who supports, preserves, sustains or vindicates.
maintenance: Sustenance; sustentation; support by means of supplies of food, clothing and other conveniences; as, his labor contributed little to the maintenance of his family.
Means of support; that which supplies conveniences. Those of better fortune not making learning their maintenance.
Support; protection; defense; vindication; as the maintenance of right or just claims.
Continuance; security from failure or decline. Whatever is granted to the church for God’s honor and the maintenance of his service, is granted to God.

In law, an officious intermeddling in a suit in which the person has no interest, by assisting either party with money or means to prosecute or defend it. This is a punishable offense. But to assist a poor kinsman from compassion, is not maintenance.
main-top: The top of the main-mast of a ship or brig.
main-yard: The yard on which the main-sail is extended, supported by the main- mast.
maiz: A plant of the genus Zea, the native corn of America, called Indian corn.
maja: A bird of Cuba, of a beautiful yellow color, whose flesh is accounted a delicacy.
majestic: August; having dignity of person or appearance; grand; princely. The prince was majestic in person and appearance. In his face . Sat meekness, hightened with majestic grace.
Splendid; grand. Get the start of this majestic world.
Elevated; lofty . The least portions must be of the epic kind; all must be grave, majestic and sublime.
Stately; becoming majesty; as a majestic air or walk.
majestically: With dignity; with grandeur; with a lofty air or appearance.
majesty: Greatness of appearance; dignity; grandeur; dignity of aspect or manner; the quality or state of a person or thing which inspires awe or reverence in the beholder; applied with peculiar propriety to God and his works. Jehovah reigneth; he is clothed with majesty. Psalm 93:7. The voice of Jehovah is full of majesty. Psalm 29:4. It is applied to the dignity, pomp and splendor of earthly princes. When he showed the riches of his glorious kingdom--the honor of his excellent majesty many days-- Esther 1:4.
Dignity; elevation of manner. The first in loftiness of thought surpass’d, The next in majesty-- .
A title of emperors, kings and queens; as most royal majesty; may it please your majesty. In this sense, it admits of the plural; as, their majesties attended the concert.
major: Greater in number, quantity or extent; as the major part of the assembly; the major part of the revenue; the major part of the territory.
Greater in dignity. My major vow lies here.

In music, an epithet applied to the modes in which the third is four semitones above the tonic or key-note, and to intervals consisting of four semitones. M. Major and minor, in music, are applied to concords which differ from each other by a semitone. Major tone, the difference between the fifth and fourth, and major semitone is the difference between the major fourth and the third. The major tone surpasses the minor by a comma.
In military affairs, an officer next in rank above a captain, and below a lieutenant colonel; the lowest field officer.

The mayor of a town. Aid-major, an officer appointed to act as major on certain occasions. Brigade-major. Drum-major, the first drummer in a regiment, who has authority over the other drummers. Fife-major, the first or chief fifer. Sergeant-major, a non-commissioned officer, subordinate to the adjutant.
major-domo: A man who holds the place of master of the house; a steward; also, a chief minister.
major-general: A military officer who commands a division or a number of regiments; the next in rank below a lieutenant general.
majority: The greater number; more than half; as a majority of mankind; a majority of votes in Congress. A measure may be carried by a large or small majority: .
Full age; the age at which the laws of a country permit a young person to manage his own affairs. Henry III. had no sooner come to his majority, than the barons raised war against him.
The office, rank or commission of a major.
The state of being greater. It is not a plurality of parts, without majority of parts.
make: To compel; to constrain. They should be made to rise at an early hour.

To form of materials; to fashion; to mold into shape; to cause to exist in a different form, or as a distinct thing. He fashioned it with a graving tool, after he had made it a molten calf. Exodus 32:4. God not only made, but created; not only made the work, but the materials.
To create; to cause to exist; to form from nothing. God made the materials of the earth and of all worlds.

To compose; to constitute as parts, materials or ingredients united in a whole. These several sums make the whole amount. The heaven, the air, the earth, and boundless sea, Make but one temple for the deity.
makebate: One who excites contention and quarrels.
maker: The Creator. The universal Maker we may praise.
One that makes, forms, shapes, or molds; a manufacturer; as a maker of watches, or of jewelry; a maker of cloth.
makepeace: A peace-maker; one that reconciles persons when are variance.
makeweight: That which is thrown into a scale to make weight.
maki: An animal of the genus Lemur. The ring-tailed maki is of the size of a cat. The common name of a subdivision of the Linnean genus Lemur, including the macauco, the mongooz, and the vari.
making: The act of forming, causing or constituting.
Workmanship. This is cloth of your own making.
mal: as a previx, in composition, denotes ill or veil, L. malus.

Extreme sinfulness; enormity or heinousness; as the malignity of sin.

manna: A substance miraculously furnished as food for the Israelites in their journey through the wilderness of Arabia. Exodus 16:4-

Josephus, Ant. B. 3.1. considers the Hebrew word man, to signify what. In conformity with this idea, the seventy translate the passage, Exodus 16:75. what is this? which rendering seems to accord with the following words, for they knew not what it was. And in the Encyclopedia, the translators are charged with making Moses fall into a plain contradiction. Art. Manna. But Christ and his apostles confirm the common version: “Not as your fathers ate manna, and are dead.” John 6:58; Hebrews 9:4. And we have other evidence, that the present version is correct; for in the same chapter, Moses directed Aaron to “take a pot and put a homer full of manna therein.” Now it would be strange language to say, put an homer full of what, or what is it. So also verse Exodus 16:35. “The children of Israel ate manna forty years” In both verses, the Hebrew word is the same as in verse Exodus 16:15.

manners: Deportment; carriage; behavior; conduct; course of life; in a moral sense. Evil communications corrupt good manners.7 Corinthians 15:33.

Ceremonious' behavior; civility; decent and respectful deportment. Shall we, in our applications to the great God, take that to be religion, which the common reason of mankind will not allow to be manners?
A bow or courtesy; as, make your manners; a popular use of the word.
mannish: Having the appearance of a man; bold; masculine; as a mannish countenance. A woman impudent and mannish grown.
manometer: An instrument to measure or show the alterations in the rarity or density of the air.
manometrical: Pertaining to the manometer; made by the manometer.
manor: The land belonging to a lord or nobleman, or so much land as a lord or great personage formerly kept in his own hands for the use and subsistence of his family. In these days, a manor rather signifies the jurisdiction and royalty incorporeal, than the land or site; for a man may have a manor in gross, as the law terms it, that is, the right and interest of a court-baron, with the perquisites thereto belonging.
manor-house: The house belonging to a manor.
manor-seat: The house belonging to a manor.
manorial: Pertaining to a manor. They have no civil liberty; their children belong not to them, but to their manorial lord.
manpleaser: One who pleases men, or one who takes uncommon pains to gain the favor of men.
manqueller: A mankiller; a manslayer; a murderer.
manse: A house or habitation; particularly, a parsonage house. A capital manse is the manor-house or lord’s court.
mansion: Any place of residence; a house; a habitation. Thy mansion wants thee, Adam. In my Father’s house are many mansions]ohn 714:2.
The house of the lord of a manor.
Residence; above. These poets near our princes sleep, And in one grave their mansions keep.
mansion-house: The house in which one resides; an inhabited house.
manslaughter: In a general sense, the killing of a man or of men; destruction of the human species; murder.

In law, the unlawful killing of a man without malice, express or implied. This may be voluntary, upon a sudden heat or excitement of anger; or involuntary, but in the commission of some unlawful act. Manslaughter differs from murder in not proceeding from malice prepense or deliberate, which is essential to constitute murder. It differs from homicide excusable, being done in consequence of some unlawful act, whereas excusable homicide happens in consequence of misadventure.
manslayer: One that has slain a human being. The Israelites had cities of refuge for manslayers.
manstealer: One who steals and sells men.
manstealing: The act of stealing a human being.
mansuete: Tame; gentle; not wild or ferocious.
manta: A flat fish that is very troublesome to pearlfishers.
mantelet: A small cloke worn by women.

In fortification, a kind of movable parapet or penthouse, made of planks, nailed one over another to the highth of almost six feet, cased with tin and set on wheels. In a siege, this is driven before pioneers, to protect them from the enemy’s small shot.
mantlet: A small cloke worn by women.

In fortification, a kind of movable parapet or penthouse, made of planks, nailed one over another to the highth of almost six feet, cased with tin and set on wheels. In a siege, this is driven before pioneers, to protect them from the enemy’s small shot.
mantiger: rather mantichor, or manticor A large monkey or baboon.
mantle: A kind of cloke or loose garment to be worn over other garments. The herald and children are clothed with mantles of satin.
Acover. Well covered with the night’s black mantle.
A cover; that which conceals; as the mantle of charity.
To cloke; to cover; to disguise. So the rising senses . Begin to chase th’ignorant fumes, that mantle . Their clearer reason.
To expand; to spread. The swan with arched neck . Between her white wings mantling, rows . Her state with oary feet.
mantle-tree: The piece of timber or stone in front of a chimney, over the fire-place, resting on the jambs.
mantle-piece: The work over a fire-place, in front of the chimney.
mantle-shelf: The work over a fire-place, in front of the chimney.
mantling: In heraldry, the representation of a mantle, or the drapery of a coat of arms.
mantology: The act or art of divination or prophesying.
mantua-maker: One who makes gowns for ladies.
manual: Performed by the hand; as manual labor or operation.
Used or made by the hand; as a deed under the king’s sign manual.
A small book, such as may be carried in the hand, or conveniently handled; as a manual of laws.
The service book of the Romish church. Manual exercise, in the military art, the exercise by which soldiers are taught the use of their muskets and other arms.
manubial: Belonging to spoils; taken in war.
manuductor: An officer in the ancient church, who gave the signal for the choir to sing, who beat time and regulated the music.
manufactory: A house or place where goods are manufactured.
manufacture: The operation of making cloth, wares, utensils, paper, books, and whatever is used by man; the operation of reducing raw materials of any kind into a form suitable for use, by the hands, by art or machinery.
Any thing made from raw materials by the hand, by machinery, or by art; as cloths, iron utensils, shoes, cabinet work, sadlery, and the like.
To make or fabricate from raw materials, by the hand, by art or machinery, and work into forms convenient for use; as, to manufacture cloth, nails, or glass.
To work raw materials into suitable forms for use; as, to manufacture wool, cotton, silk or iron.
manufactured: Made form raw materials into forms for use.
manufacturer: One who works raw materials into wares suitable for use.
One who employs workmen for manufacturing; the owner of a manufactory.
manufacturing: Making goods and wares from raw materials.
manumission: The act of liberating a slave from bondage, and giving him freedom.
manumit: é. To release from slavery; to liberate from personal bondage or servitude; to free, as a slave.
manurable: That may be cultivated. This, though the original sense, is rarely or never used. The present sense of manure, would give the following signification.
That may be manured, or enriched by manure.
manure: To cultivate by manual labor; to till: .
To apply to land any fertilizing matter, as dung, compost, ashes, lime, fish, or any vegetable or animal substance.
To fertilize; to enrich with nutritive substances. The corps of half her senate' . Manure the fields of Thessaly.

Any matter which fertilizes land, as the contents of stables and barnyards, marl, ashes, fish, salt, and every kind of animal and vegetable substance applied to land, or capable of furnishing nutriment to plants.
manured: Dressed or overspread with a fertilizing substance.
manuring: Dressing or overspreading land with manure; fertilizing.
A dressing or spread of manure on land.
manuscript: A book or paper written with the hand or pen.
Written with the hand; not printed.
many: Numerous; comprising a great number of individuals. Thou shalt be a father of many nations. Genesis 17:4. Not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called. 7 Corinthians 1:26. Many are the afflictions of the righteous. Psalm 34:19. It is often preceded by as or so, and followed by so, indicating an equal number. As many books as you take, so many shall be charged to your account. So many laws argue so many sins. It is followed by as. As many as were willing-hearted brought bracelets. Exodus 35:22. It precedes an or a, before a noun in the singular number. Full many a gem of purest ray serene.
In low language, preceded by too, it denotes powerful or much; as, they are too many for us. MAN Y.
men’ny. A multitude; a great number of individuals; the people. O thou fond many. The vulgar and the many are fit only to be led or driven.
men’ny. A retinue of servants; household.
many-cornered: Having many corners, or more than twelve; polygonal.
many-headed: Having many heads; as a many-headed monster; many-headed tyranny.
many-parted: Multipartite; divided into several parts; as a corol.
map: A representation of the surface of the earth or of any part of it, drawn on paper or other material, exhibiting the lines of latitude and longitude, and the positions of countries, kingdoms, states, mountains, riversA map of the earth, or of a large portion of it, comprehends a representation of land and water; but a representation of a continent or any portion of land only, is properly a map, and a representation of the ocean only or any portion of it, is called a chart. We say, a map of England, of France, of Europe; but a chart of the Atlantic, of the Pacific
To draw or delineate, as the figure of any portion of land.
maple: A tree of the genus Acer, of several species. Of the sap of the rock maple, sugar is made in America, in great quantities, by evaporation.
maple-tree: A tree of the genus Acer, of several species. Of the sap of the rock maple, sugar is made in America, in great quantities, by evaporation.
maple-sugar: Sugar obtained by evaporation from the juice of the rock maple.
mappery: The art of planning and designing maps.
mar: To injure by cutting off a part, or by wounding and making defective; as, to mar a tree by incision. I pray you, mar no more trees by writing songs in their barks. Neither shalt thou mar the corners of thy beard. Leviticus 19:27.
To injure; to hurt; to impair the strength or purity of. When brewers mar their malt with water.
To injure; to diminish; to interrupt. But mirth is marred, and the good cheer is lost.

To injure; to deform; to disfigure. Ire, envy and despair . Marr’d all his borrow’d visage. His visage was so marred more than any man. Isaiah 52:14. Moral evil alone mars the intellectual works of God.
maracan: A species of parrot in Brazil.
maracock: A plant of the genus Passiflora.
maranatha: The Lord comes or has come; a word used by the apostle Paul in expressing a curse. This word was used in anathematizing persons for great crimes; as much as to say, “may the Lord come quickly to take vengeance on thee for thy crimes.”
maranon: The proper name of a river in South America, the largest in the world; most absurdly called Amazon.
marasmus: Atrophy; a wasting of flesh without fever or apparent disease; a kind of consumption.
maraud: To rove in quest of plunder; to make an excursion for booty; to plunder.
marauder: A rover in quest of booty or plunder; a plunderer; usually applied to small parties of soldiers.
marauding: A roving for plunder; a plundering by invaders.
maravedi: A small copper coin of Spain, equal to three mills American money, less than a farthing sterling.
marble: To variegate in color; to cloud; to stain or vein like marble; as, to marble the cover of abook.

The popular name of any species of calcarious stone or mineral, of a compact texture, and of a beautiful appearance, susceptible of a good polish. The varieties are numerous, and greatly diversified in color. Marble is limestone, or a stone which may be calcined to lime, a carbonate of lime; but limestone is a more general name, comprehending the calcarious stones of an inferior texture, as well as those which admit a fine polish. Marble is much used for statues, busts, pillars, chimney pieces, monuments.
A little ball of marble or other stone, used by children in play.

A stone remarkable for some inscription or sculpture. Arundel marbles, Arundelian marbles, marble pieces with a chronicle of the city of Athens inscribed on them; presented to the university of Oxford, by Thomas, earl of Arundel.
Made of marble; as a marble pillar.
marbled: Diversified in color; veined like marble.
marbled-hearted: 2. Having a heart like marble; hard hearted; cruel; insensible; incapable of being moved by pity, love or sympathy.
marbling: Variegating in colors; clouding or veining like marble.
The art or practice of variegating in color, in imitation of marble.
marcasite: A name which has been given to all sorts of minerals, to ores, pyrites, and semi-metals. It is now obsolete.
marcasitic: Pertaining to marcasite; of the nature of marcasite.
marcessible: That may wither; liable to decay.
march: The third month of the year.
To border on; to be contiguous to.
To move by steps and in order, as soldiers; to move in a military manner. We say, the army marched, or the troops marched.
To walk in a grave, deliberate or stately manner. Like thee, great son of Jove, like thee, When clad in rising majesty, Thou marchest down o’er Delos’ hills.
To cause to move, as an army. Buonaparte marched an immense army to Moscow, but he did not march them back to France.
marcher: The lord or officer who defended the marches or borders of a territory.
marches: Borders; limits; confines; as lord of the marches.
marching: Moving or walking in order or in a stately manner.
marchioness: The wife or widow of a marquis; or a female having the rank and dignity of a marquis.
marchpane: A kind of sweet bread or biscuit.2z
marcor: The state of withering or wasting; leanness; waste of flesh.
mare: The female of the horse, or equine genus of quadrupeds. A kind or torpor or stagnation which seems to press the stomach in sleep; the incubus.
mareca: A species of duck in South America.
marena: A kind of fish somewhat like a pilchard.
mareschal: m‘arshal. The chief commander of an army.
margarate: In chimistry, a compound of margaric acid with a base.
margaric: Pertaining to pearl. The margaric acid is obtained by digesting soap made of hog’s lard and potash, in water. It appears in the form of pearly scales.
margarin: A peculiar pearl-like substance, extracted from hog’s lard; called also margarite and margaric acid.
margarine: A peculiar pearl-like substance, extracted from hog’s lard; called also margarite and margaric acid.
margarite: A mineral of a grayish white color found in Tyrol.
margay: An American animal of the cat kind.
margin: A border; edge; brink; verge; as the margin of a river or lake.
The edge of the leaf or page of a book, left blank or filled with notes.
In botany, the edge of a leaf.
é. To furnish with a margin; to border.
marginal: Written or printed in the margin; as a marginal note or gloss.
marginally: In the margin of a book.
margode: A bluish gray stone, resembling clay in external appearance, but so hard as to cut spars and zeolites.
margot: A fish of the perch kind, found in the waters of Carolina.
margrave: Originally, a lord or keeper of the marches or borders; now a title of nobility in Germany
margraviate: The territory or jurisdiction of a margrave.
marigenous: Produced in or by the sea.
marigold: A plant of the genus Calendula, bearing a yellow flower. There are several plants of different genera bearing this name; as the African marigold, of the genus Tagetes; corn-marigold, of the genus Chrysanthemum; fig-marigold, of the genus Mesembryanthemum; marsh-marigold, of the genus Caltha.
marikin: A species of monkey having a mane.
marinate: To salt or pickle fish, and then preserve them in oil or vinegar.
marine: Pertaining to the sea; as marine productions or bodies; marine shells.
Transacted at sea; done on the ocean; as a marine engagement.
Doing duty on the sea; as a marine officer; marine forces.
A soldier that serves on board of a ship in naval engagements. In the plural, marines, a body of troops trained to do military service on board of ships.
The whole navy of a kingdom or state.
mariner: A seaman or sailor; one whose occupation is to assist in navigating ships.
mariput: The zoril, an animal of the skunk tribe.
marish: Low ground, wet or covered with water and coarse grass; a fen; a bog; a moor. It is now written marsh, which see.
maritime: Relating or pertaining to the sea or ocean; as maritime affairs.
Performed on the sea; naval; as maritime service.
Bordering on the sea; as a maritime coast.
Situated near the sea; as maritime towns.

Having a navy and commerce by sea; as maritime powers. Maritimal is not now used. [Note. We never say, a maritime body, a maritime shell or production, a maritime officer or engagement, a maritime league. See Marine.; .
marjoram: A plant of the genus Origanum, of several species. The sweet marjoram is peculiarly aromatic and fragrant, and much used in cookery. The Spanish marjoram is of the genus Urtica.
mark: A visible line made by drawing one substance on another; as a mark made by chalk or charcoal, or a pen.
A line, groove or depression made by stamping or cutting; an incision; a channel or impression; as the mark of a chisel, of a stamp, of a rod or whip; the mark of the finger or foot.
Any note or sign of distinction. The Lord set a mark upon Cain. Genesis 4:15.
Any visible effect of force or agency. There are scarce any marks left of a subterraneous fire.
Any apparent or intelligible effect; proof. The confusion of tongues was a mark of separation.
marked: Impressed with any note or figure of distinction; noted; distinguished by some character.
marker: One who puts a mark on any thing.
One that notes or takes notice.
market: A public place in a city or town, where provisions or cattle are exposed to sale; an appointed place for selling and buying at private sale, a distinguished from an auction.
A public building in which provisions are exposed to sale; a market-house.

Sale; the exchange of provisions or goods for money; purchase or rate of purchase and sale. The seller says he comes to a bad market, when the buyer says he comes to a good market. We say, the markets are low or high; by which we understand the price or rate of purchase. We say that commodities find a quick or ready market; markets are dull. We are not able to find a market for our goods or provisions.
Place of sale; as the British market; the American market.
The privilege of keeping a public market.
market-bell: The bell that gives notice of the time or day of market.
market-cross: A cross set up where a market is held.
market-day: The day of a public market.
market-folks: People that come to the market.
market-house: A building for a public market.
market-maid: A woman that brings things to market.
market-man: A man that brings things to market.
market-place: The place where provisions or goods are exposed to sale.
market-price: The current price of commodities at any given time.
market-rate: The current price of commodities at any given time.
market-town: A town that has the privilege of a stated public market.
market-woman: A woman that brings things to market or that attends a market for selling any thing.
marketable: Current in market; as marketable value.
marksman: One that is skillful to hit a mark; he that shoots well.
One who, not able to write, makes his mark instead of his name.
marl: A species of calcarious earth, of different composition, being united with clay or fuller’s earth. In a crude state, it effervesces with acids. It is found loose and friable, or more or less indurated. It possesses fertilizing properties and is much used for manure. Marl is composed of carbonate of lime and clay in various proportions.
To overspread or manure with marl.
marlaceous: Resembling marl; partaking of the qualities of marl.
marline: A small line composed of two strands little twisted, and either tarred or white; used for winding round ropes and cables, to prevent their being fretted by the blocks
marline-spike: A small iron like a large spike, used to open the bolt rope when the sail is to be sewed to it
marling: The act of winding a small line about a rope, to prevent its being galled.
marlitic: Partaking of the qualities of marlite.
marlpit: A pit where marl is dug.
marly: Consisting in or partaking of marl.
marmalade: The pulp of quinces boiled into a consistence with sugar, or a confection of plums, apricots, quincesboiled with sugar. In Scotland, it is made of Seville oranges and sugar only.
marmalite: A mineral of a pearly or metallic luster; a hydrate of magnesia.
marmoration: A covering or incrusting with marble.
marmose: An animal resembling the opossum, but less. Instead of a bag, this animal has two longitudinal folds near the thighs, which serve to inclose the young.
marmot: A quadruped of the genus Arctomys, allied to the murine tribe. It is about the size of the rabbit, and inhabits the higher region of the Alps and Pyrenees. The name is also given to other species of the genus. The woodchuck of North America is called the Maryland marmot.
maroon: A name given to free blacks living on the mountains in the West India isles.
To put a sailor a shore on a desolate isle, under pretense of his having committed some great crime.
marque: Letters of marque are letters of reprisal; a license or extraordinary commission granted by a sovereign of one state to his subjects, to make reprisals at sea on the subjects of another, under pretense of indemnification for injuries received. Marque is said to be from the same root as marches, limits, frontiers, and literally to denote a license to pass the limits of a jurisdiction on land, for the purpose of obtaining satisfaction for theft by seizing the property of the subjects of a foreign nation. I can give no better account of the origin of this word.
The ship commissioned for making reprisals.
marquetry: Inlaid work; work inlaid with variegations of fine wood, shells, ivory and the like.
marquis: A title of honor in Great Britain, next to that of duke. Originally, the marquis was an officer whose duty was to guard the marches or frontiers of the kingdom. The office has ceased, and marquis is now a mere title conferred by patent.
marquisate: The seigniory, dignity, or lordship of a marquis.
marrer: One that mars, hurts or impairs.
marriage: The act of uniting a man and woman for life; wedlock; the legal union of a man and woman for life. Marriage is a contract both civil and religious, by which the parties engage to live together in mutual affection and fidelity, till death shall separate them. Marriage was instituted by God himself for the purpose of preventing the promiscuous intercourse of the sexes, for promoting domestic felicity, and for securing the maintenance and education of children. Marriage is honorable in all and the bed undefiled. Hebrews 13:4.
A feast made on the occasion of a marriage. The kingdom of heaven is like a certain king, who made a marriage for his son. Matthew 22:2.
In a scriptural sense, the union between Christ and his church by the covenant of grace. Revelation 19:7.
marriageable: Of an age suitable for marriage; fit to be married. Young persons are marriageable at an earlier age in warm climates than in cold.
marriage-articles: Contract or agreement on which a marriage is founded.
married: a. Conjugal; connubial; as the married state.
marrow: A soft oleaginous substance contained in the cavities of animal bones.
In the Scottish dialect, a companion; fellow; associate; match.
To fill with marrow or with fat; to glut.
marrow-bone: A bone containing marrow, or boiled for its marrow.
The bone of the knee; in ludicrous language.
marry: To unite in wedlock or matrimony; to join a man and woman for life, and constitute them man and wife according to the laws or customs of a nation. By the laws, ordained clergymen have a right to marry persons within certain limits prescribed. Tell him he shall marry the couple himself.
To dispose of in wedlock. Mecaenas told Augustus he must either marry his daughter Julia to Agrippa, or take away his life.
To take for husband or wife. We say, a man marries a woman; or a woman marries a man. The first was the original sense, but both are now well authorized.
In Scripture, to unite in covenant, or in the closest connection. Turn, O backsliding children, saith Jehovah, for I am married to you. Jeremiah 3:14.

To enter into the conjugal state; to unite as husband and wife; to take a husband or a wife. If the case of the man be so with his wife, it is not good to marry. Matthew 19:10. I will therefore that the younger women marry. 7 Timothy 5:14.
mars: In mythology, the god of war; in modern usage, a planet; and in the old chimistry, a term for iron.
marsh: A tract of low land, usually or occasionally covered with water, or very wet and miry, and overgrown with coarse grass or with detached clumps of sedge; a fen. It differs from swamp, which is merely moist or spungy land, but often producing valuable crops of grass. Lowland occasionally overflowed by the tides, is called salt marsh.
marsh-elder: The gelder rose, a species of Viburnum.
marsh-mallow: A plant of the genus Althaea.
marsh-marigold: A plant of the genus Caltha.
marshal: The chief officer of arms, whose duty it is to regulate combats in the lists.
One who regulates rank and order at a feast or any other assembly, directs the order of procession and the like.
A harbinger; a pursuivant; one who goes before a prince to declare his coming and provide entertainment.
In France, the highest military officer. In other countries of Europe, a marshal is a military officer of high rank, and called field- marshal.

In America, a civil officer, appointed by the President and Senate of the United States, in each judicial district, answering to the sheriff of a county. His duty is to execute all precepts directed to him, issued under the authority of the United States.6E
marshaler: One who disposes in due order.
marshalsea: In England, the prison in Southwark, belonging to the marshal of the king’s household. Court of marshalsea, a court formerly held before the steward and marshal of the king’s house, to administer justice between the king’s domestic servants.
marshy: Produced in marshes; as a marshy weed.
mart: A place of sale or traffick. It was formerly applied chiefly to markets and fairs in cities and towns, but it has now a more extensive application. We say, the United States are a principal mart for English goods; England and France are the marts of American cotton.
To buy and sell; to traffick.
marten: An animal of the genus Mustela, or weasel kind, whose fur is used in making hats and muffs.
martial: Pertaining to war; suited to war; as martial equipage; martial music; a martial appearance.
Warlike; brave; given to war; as a martial nation or people.
Suited to battle; as a martial array.
Belonging to war, or to an army and navy; opposed to civil; as martial law; a court martial.
Pertaining to Mars, or borrowing the properties of that planet. The natures of the fixed start are esteemed martial or jovial, according to the colors by which they answer to those planets.
martin: A bird of the genus Hirundo, which forms its nest in buildings. It was formerly written by some authors martlet.
martinet: In military language, a strict disciplinarian; so called from an officer of that name.
martlet: In military language, a strict disciplinarian; so called from an officer of that name.

Martlets, in heraldry, are little birds represented without feed, used as a mark of distinction for younger brothers of a family, who are thus admonished that they are to trust for promotion to the wings of merit.
martinets: In ships, martinets are small lines fastened to the leech of a sail, to bring it close to the yard when the sail is furled.
martingal: A strap or thong fastened to the girth under a horse’s belly, and at the other end to the muss-roll, passing between the fore legs.
In ships, a rope extending from the jibboom, to the end of a bumpkin under the cap of the bowsprit.
martinmas: The feast of St. Martin, the eleventh of November.
martyr: One who, by his death, bears witness to the truth of the gospel. Stephen was the first christian martyr. To be a martyr signifies only to witness the truth of Christ.
One who suffers death in defense of any cause. We say, a man dies a martyr to his political principles or to the cause of liberty: .
To put to death for adhering to what one believes to be the truth; to sacrifice one on account of his faith or profession.
martyrdom: The death of a martyr; the suffering of death on account of one’s adherence to the faith of the gospel. He intends to crown their innocence with the glory of martyrdom.
martyrological: Registering or registered in a catalogue or martyrs.
martyrologist: A writer of martyrology, or an account of martyrs.
martyrology: A history or account of martyrs with their sufferings; or a register of martyrs.
marvel: A wonder; that which arrests the attention and causes a person to stand or gaze, or to pause. [This word is nearly obsolete, or at least little used in elegant writings.,
Wonder; admiration. Marvel of Peru, a plant of the genus Mirabilis.
To wonder. It expresses less than astonish or amaze.
marvelous: Wonderful; strange; exciting wonder or some degree of surprise. This is the Lord’s doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. Psalm 118:23.
The marvelous, in writings, is that which exceeds natural power, or is preternatural; opposed to probable.
marvelously: Wonderfully; strangely; in a manner to excite wonder or surprise.
mascle: m‘asl. In heraldry, a lozenge, as it were perforated.
masculine: Having the qualities of a man; strong; robust; as a masculine body.
Resembling man; coarse; opposed to delicate or soft; as masculine features.
Bold; brave; as a masculine spirit or courage.
In grammar, the masculine gender of words is that which expresses a male, or something analogous to it; or it is the gender appropriated to males, though not always expressing the male sex.
masculineness: The quality or state of being manly; resemblance of man in qualities; as in coarseness of features, strength of body, boldness
mash: A mixture or mass of ingredients, beaten or blended together in a promiscuous manner.
To beat into a confused mass.
To bruise; to crush by beating or pressure; as, to mash apples in a mill.
mashed: Beat into a mass; bruised; crushed; mixed into a mash.
mashing: Beating into a mass; bruising; crushing.
mashing-tub: A tub for containing the mash in breweries.
mask: A cover for the face; that which conceals the face, especially a cover with apertures for the eyes and mouth; a visor. A mask is designed to conceal the face from beholders, or to preserve the complexion from injury by exposure to the weather and the rays of the sun.
That which disguises; any pretense or subterfuge.
A festive entertainment of dancing or other diversions, in which the company all wear masks; a masquerade.
A revel; a bustle; a piece of mummery. This thought might lead through this world’s vain mask.
A dramatic performance written in a tragic style, without attention to rules or probability: .
masked: Having the face covered; concealed; disguised.
masker: One that wears a mask; one that plays the fool at a masquerade.
maskery: The dress or disguise of a masker.
mason: A man whose occupation is to lay bricks and stones, or to construct the walls of buildings, chimneys and the like, which consist of bricks or stones.
Amember of the fraternity of free masons.
masonic: Pertaining to the craft or mysteries of free masons.
masonry: The art or occupation of a mason.
The work or performance of a mason; as when we say, the wall is good masonry.
masora: A Hebrew work on the bible, by several Rabbins.
masoretic: Relating to the Masorites, who interpreted the Scriptures by tradition, and invented the Hebrew points to fix the true reading and pronunciation. Whence the vowel points are denominated masoretic.
masorite: One of the writers of the Masora.
masquerade: A nocturnal assembly of persons wearing masks, and amusing themselves with dancing, conversation and other diversions. In courtly balls and midnight masquerades.N .
Disguise. I came to visit thee in masquerade.
masquerader: A person wearing a mask; one disguised.
mass: A lump; a body of matter concreted, collected or formed into a lump; applied to any solid body; as a mass of iron or lead; a mass of flesh; as mass of ice; a mass of dough.
A collective body of fluid matter. The ocean is a mass of water.
A heap; as a mass of earth.
A great quantity collected; as a mass of treasure.
Bulk; magnitude. This army of such mass and charge.
massacer: The murder of an individual, or the slaughter of numbers of human beings, with circumstances of cruelty; the indiscriminate killing of human beings, without authority or necessity, and without forms civil or military. It differs from assassination, which is a private killing. It differs from carnage, which is rather the effect of slaughter than slaughter itself, and is applied to the authorized destruction of men in battle. Massacre is sometimes called butchery, from its resemblance to the killing of cattle. If a soldier kills a man in battle in his own defense, it is a lawful act; it is killing, and it is slaughter, but it is not a massacre. Whereas, if a soldier kills an enemy after he has surrendered, it is a massacre, a killing without necessity, often without authority, contrary to the usages of nations, and of course with cruelty. The practice of killing prisoners, even when author
To murder human beings with circumstances of cruelty; to kill men with indiscriminate violence, without authority or necessity, and contrary to the usages of nations; to butcher human beings.
massacre: The murder of an individual, or the slaughter of numbers of human beings, with circumstances of cruelty; the indiscriminate killing of human beings, without authority or necessity, and without forms civil or military. It differs from assassination, which is a private killing. It differs from carnage, which is rather the effect of slaughter than slaughter itself, and is applied to the authorized destruction of men in battle. Massacre is sometimes called butchery, from its resemblance to the killing of cattle. If a soldier kills a man in battle in his own defense, it is a lawful act; it is killing, and it is slaughter, but it is not a massacre. Whereas, if a soldier kills an enemy after he has surrendered, it is a massacre, a killing without necessity, often without authority, contrary to the usages of nations, and of course with cruelty. The practice of killing prisoners, even when author
To murder human beings with circumstances of cruelty; to kill men with indiscriminate violence, without authority or necessity, and contrary to the usages of nations; to butcher human beings.
masseter: A muscle which raises the under jaw.
massicot: Calcined white lead; yellow oxyd of lead. Lead exposed to the air while melting, is covered with a gray, dusky pellicle. This pellicle carefully taken off, is reduced by agitation to a greenish gray powder, inclining to yellow. This oxyd, separated from the grains of lead by sifting, and exposed to a more intense heat, sufficient to make it red hot, assumes a deep yellow color. In this state it is called massicot. Massicot, slowly heated by a moderate fire, takes a beautiful red color, and obtains the name of minium. Massicot is sometimes used by painters, and it is used as a drier in the composition of ointments and plasters.
masticot: Calcined white lead; yellow oxyd of lead. Lead exposed to the air while melting, is covered with a gray, dusky pellicle. This pellicle carefully taken off, is reduced by agitation to a greenish gray powder, inclining to yellow. This oxyd, separated from the grains of lead by sifting, and exposed to a more intense heat, sufficient to make it red hot, assumes a deep yellow color. In this state it is called massicot. Massicot, slowly heated by a moderate fire, takes a beautiful red color, and obtains the name of minium. Massicot is sometimes used by painters, and it is used as a drier in the composition of ointments and plasters.
massiness: The state of being massy; great weight or weight with bulk; ponderousness.
massiveness: The state of being massy; great weight or weight with bulk; ponderousness.
massive: Heavy; weighty; ponderous; bulky and heavy; as a massy shield; a massy rock. The yawning rocks in massy fragments fly.
In mineralogy, in mass; having a crystalline structure, but not a regular form. We say, a mineral occurs massive.
massy: Heavy; weighty; ponderous; bulky and heavy; as a massy shield; a massy rock. The yawning rocks in massy fragments fly.
mast: A long, round piece of timber, elevated or designed to be raised perpendicularly or nearly so, on the keel of a ship or other vessel, to which the yards, sails and rigging are attached, and by which they are supported. A mast is a single stick, formed from the trunk of a tree, or it consists of many pieces of timber united by iron bands. Masts are of several kinds, as the main-mast, fore-mast, mizzen-mast, top-mast, top-gallant-mast
The fruit of the oak and beech, or other forest trees; nuts; acorns.
masted: Furnished with a mast or masts.
master: Aman who rules, governs or directs either men or business. A man who owns slaves is their master; he who has servants is their master; he who has apprentices is their master; he who has apprentices is their master, as he has the government and direction of them. The man who superintends and directs any business, is master, or master workman. O thou my friend, my genius, come along, Thou master of the poet and the song. Nations that want protectors, will have masters.
A director, head, or chief manager; as the master of a feast.

The owner; proprietor; with the idea of governing. The master of a house may be the owner, or the occupant, who has a temporary right of governing it. It would be believed that he rather took the horse for his subject, than his master.
A lord; a ruler; one who has supreme dominion. Caesar, the world’s great master and his own.
A chief; a principal; as the master root of a plant. One master passion swallows up the rest.
masterful: Having the skill of a master; also, imperious; arbitrary.
master-hand: The hand of a man eminently skillful.
master-key: The key that opens many locks, the subordinate keys of which open only one each.
masterless: Destitute of a master or owner.
master-lode: In mining, the principal vein of ore.
masterly: Formed or executed with superior skill; suitable to a master; most excellent; skillful; as a masterly design; a masterly performance; a masterly stroke of policy.
With the skill of a master. Thou dost speak masterly.
master-piece: A capital performance; any thing done or made with superior or extraordinary skill. This wondrous master-piece I fain would see. ,
Chief excellence or talent. Dissimulation was his master-piece. WASTER-PIECE.
mastership: Superiority; preeminence. Where noble youths for mastership should — strive.
Title of respect; in irony. How now, signor Launce, what new with your mastership.
master-sinew: A large sinew that surrounds the hough of a horse, and divides it from the bone by a hollow place, where the wind-galls are usually seated.
master-wort: A plant of the genus Imperatoria.
mastery: Dominion; power of governing or commanding. If divided by mountains, they will fight for the mastery of the passages of the tops-- .
Superiority in competition; preeminence. Every man that striveth for the mystery, is temperate in all things. Corinthians 9:25.
Victory in war. It is not the voice of them that shout for mastery. Exodus 32:18.
Eminent skill; superior dexterity: . He could attain to a mastery in all languages.
Attainment of eminent skill or power. The learning and mastery of a tongue being unpleasant in itself, should not be cumbered with other difficulties.
mastful: Abounding with mast, or fruit of oak, beech and other forest trees; as the mastful chestnut.
mastic: A resin exuding from the mastic-tree, a species of Pistacia, and obtained by incision. It is in white farinaceous tears, of a faint smell, and is used as an astringent and an aromatic. It is used also as an ingredient in drying varnishes.
mastich: A resin exuding from the mastic-tree, a species of Pistacia, and obtained by incision. It is in white farinaceous tears, of a faint smell, and is used as an astringent and an aromatic. It is used also as an ingredient in drying varnishes.
masticate: To chew; to grind with the teeth and prepare for swallowing and digestion; as, to masticate food.
masticating: Chewing; breaking into small pieces with the teeth.
mastication: The act or operation of chewing solid food, breaking it into small pieces, and mixing it with saliva; thus preparing it for deglutition, and more easy digestion in the stomach. Mastication is a necessary preparation of solid aliment, without which there can be no good digestion.
masticatory: Chewing; adapted to perform the office of chewing food.
A substance to be chewed to increase the saliva.
mastiff: mastiffs. Mastives is irregular. A large species of dog, remarkable for strength and courage. Strabo informs us that the mastiffs of Britain were trained for war, and used by the Gauls in battle.
mastless: Having no mast; as a vessel.
Bearing no mast; as a mastless oak or beech.
mastodon: A genus of mammiferous animals resembling the elephant, now extinct, and known only by their fossil remains. It includes the N. American mammoth.
mastoid: Resembling the nipple or breast; as the mastoid muscle; the mastoid process.
masty: Full of mast; abounding with acorns
mat: A texture of sedge, rushes, flags, husks, straw, or other material, to be laid on a floor for cleaning the boots and shoes of those who enter a house, and for other purposes.
A web of rope-yard, used in ships to secure the standing rigging from the friction of the yards.
To cover or lay with mats.
To twist together; to interweave like a mat; to entangle. And o’er his eyebrows hung his matted hair.
To press together; to lay flat; as matted grass.
matadore: One of the three principal cards in the game of omber and quadrille, which are always two black aces and the deuce in spades and clubs, and the seven in hearts and diamonds.
match: Some very combustible substance used for catching fire from a spark, as hemp, flax, cotton, tow dipped in sulphur, or a species of dry wood, called vulgarly touch-wood.

A rope or cord made of hempen tow, composed of three strands slightly twisted, and again covered with tow and boiled in the lees of old wine. This when lighted at one end, retains fire and burns slowly till consumed. It is used in firing artillery.
A person who is equal to another in strength or other quality; one able to cope with another. Government--makes an innocent man of the lowest ranks a match for the mightiest of his fellow subjects.
One that suits or tallies with another; or any thing that equals another.
Union by marriage. Love doth seldom suffer itself to be confined by other matches than those of its own making. In popular language, it is applied to the engagement of lovers before marriage.
matchable: Equal; suitable; fit to be joined.
matched: Equaled; suited; placed in opposition; married.
matching: Equaling; suiting; setting in opposition; uniting in marriage.
matchless: Having no equal; as matchless impudence; a matchless queen; matchless love or charms.
matchlessly: In a manner or degree not to be equaled.
matchlessness: The state or quality of being without an equal.
matchlock: Formerly, the lock of a musket which was fired by a match.
matchmaker: One who makes matches for burning.
One who contrives or effects a union by marriage.
materialism: The doctrine of materialists; the opinion of those who maintain that the soul of man is not a spiritual substance distinct from matter, but that it is the result or effect or the organization of matter in the body. The irregular fears of a future state had been supplanted by the materialism of Epicurus.
materialist: One who denies the existence of spiritual substances, and maintains that the soul of man is the result of a particular organization of matter in the body.
materiality: Importance; as the materiality of facts.
materialize: To reduce to a state of matter; also, to regard as matter.
materially: Not formally; substantially. An ill intention may spoil an act materially good.
In an important manner or degree; essentially. It materially concerns us to know the real motives of our actions.
maternal: Motherly; pertaining to a mother; becoming a mother; as maternal love; maternal tenderness.
maternity: The character or relation of a mother.
matfelon: A plant of the genus Centaurea, knap-weed.
mathematic: Pertaining to mathematics; as mathematical knowledge; mathematical instruments.
According to the principles of mathematics; as mathematical exactness.
mathematical: Pertaining to mathematics; as mathematical knowledge; mathematical instruments.
According to the principles of mathematics; as mathematical exactness.
mathematically: According to the laws or principles of mathematical science.
mathematics: The science of quantity; the science which treats of magnitude and number, or of whatever can be measured or numbered. This science is divided into pure or speculative, which considers quantity abstractly, without relation to matter; and mixed, which treats of magnitude as subsisting in material bodies, and is consequently interwoven with physical considerations. It is the peculiar excellence of mathematics, that its principles are demonstrable. Arithmetic, geometry, algebra, trigonometry, and conic sections, are branches of mathematics.
mathemeg: A fish of the cod kind, inhabiting Hudson’s bay.
matin: Pertaining to the morning; used in the morning; as a matin trumpet.
matins: Morning worship or service; morning prayers or songs. The vigils are celebrated before them, and the nocturne and matins, for the saints whose the relics are. The winged choristers began . To chirp their matins.
Time of morning service; the first canonical hour in the Romish church.
matrass: A cucurbit; a chimical vessel in the shape of an egg, or with a tapering neck, open at the top, serving the purposes of digestion, evaporation
matress: A quilted bed; a bed stuffed with hair, moss or other soft material, and quilted.
matrice: The womb; the cavity in which the fetus of an animal is formed and nourished till its birth.
A mold; the cavity in which any thing is formed, and which gives it shape; as the matrix of a type.
The place where any thing is formed or produced; as the matrix of metals; gang.
In dyeing, the five simple colors, black, white, blue, red and yellow, of which all the rest are composed.
matrix: The womb; the cavity in which the fetus of an animal is formed and nourished till its birth.
A mold; the cavity in which any thing is formed, and which gives it shape; as the matrix of a type.
The place where any thing is formed or produced; as the matrix of metals; gang.
In dyeing, the five simple colors, black, white, blue, red and yellow, of which all the rest are composed.
matricide: The killing or murder of a mother.
