metonic year: the cycle of the moon, or period of nineteen years, in which the lunations of the moon return to the same days of the month; so called from its discoverer Meton the Athenian.
metonymic: Used by way of metonymy, by putting one word for another.
metonymical: Used by way of metonymy, by putting one word for another.
metonymically: By putting one word for another.
metonymy: In rhetoric, a trope in which one word is put for another; a change of names which have some relation to each other; as when we say, “a man keeps a good table.” instead of good provisions. “We read Virgil.” that is, his poems or writings. “They have Moses and the prophets,” that is, their books or writings. A man has a clear head, that is, understanding, intellect; a warm heart, that is affections.
metope: met’opy. In architecture, the space between the triglyphs of the Doric frieze, which among the ancients used to be painted or adorned with carved work.
metoposcopy: The study of physiognomy; the art of discovering the character or the dispositions of men by their features, or the lines of the face.
metrical: Pertaining to measure, or due arrangement or combination of long and short syllables.
Consisting of verses; as metrical compositions.
metrology: A discourse on measures or mensuration; the description of measures.
An account of measures, or the science of weights and measures.
metropolis: Literally, the mother-city, that is, the chief city or capital of a kingdom, state or country, as Paris in France, Madrid in Spain, London in Great Britain. In the United States, Washington, in the District of Columbia, is the metropolis, as being the seat of government; but in several of the states, the largest cities are not the seats of the respective governments. Yet New York city, in the state of that name, and Philadelphia in Pennsylvania, are the chief cities, and may be called each the metropolis of the state in which it is situated, though neither of them is the seat of government in the state.
metropolitan: Belonging to a metropolis, or to the mother church; residing in the chief city.
The bishop of the mother church; an archbishop.
metropolitic: Pertaining to a metropolis; chief or principal of cities; archiepiscopal.
metropolitical: Pertaining to a metropolis; chief or principal of cities; archiepiscopal.
mettle: met'l. Spirit; constitutional ardor; that temperament which is susceptible of high excitement. It is not synonymous with courage, though it may be accompanied with it, and is sometimes used for it.

met’l. Spirit; constitutional ardor; that temperament which is susceptible of high excitement. It is not synonymous with courage, though it may be accompanied with it, and is sometimes used for it. The winged courser, like a generous horse, Shows most true mettle when you check his course.
mettled: High spirited; ardent; full of fire.
mettlesome: Full of spirit; possessing constitutional ardor; brisk; fiery; as a mettlesome horse.
mettlesomeness: The state of being high spirited.
mew: A seafowl of the genus Larus; a gull.
A cage for birds; an inclosure; a place of confinement.
To shut up; to inclose; to confine, as in a cage or other inclosure. More pity that the eagle should be mew’d. Close mew’‘d in their sedans, for fear of air.
To shed or cast; to change; to molt. The hawk mewed his feathers. Nine times the moon had mew’d her horns-- .
mewing: Casting the feathers or skin; crying.
mewl: To cry or squall, as a child.
mezereon: A plant of the genus Daphne; the spurge olive.
mezzotinto: A particular manner of engraving or representation of figures on copper, in imitation of painting in Indian ink. To perform this the plate is scratched and furrowed in different directions; the design is then drawn on the face, then the dents and furrows are erased from the parts where the lights of the piece are to be; the parts which are to represent shades being left.
miasm: Infecting substances floating in the air; the effluvia or fine particles of any putrefying bodies, rising and floating in the atmosphere, and considered to be noxious to health.
miasma: Infecting substances floating in the air; the effluvia or fine particles of any putrefying bodies, rising and floating in the atmosphere, and considered to be noxious to health.
miasmatic: Pertaining to miasma; partaking of the qualities of noxious effluvia.
mica: A mineral of a foliated structure, consisting of thin flexible lamels or scales, having a shining surface. The scales are sometimes parallel, sometimes interwoven, sometimes wavy or undulated, sometimes representing filaments. It is called also talck, glimmer, muscovy-glass, and glist. Jameson subdivides mica into ten subspeciesmica, pinite, lepidolite, chlorite, green earth, talck, nacrite, potstone, steatite and figure stone.
micaceous: Pertaining to mica; resembling mica or partaking of its properties.
micarel: A species of argillaceous earth; a mineral of a brownish or blackish red color, commonly crystallized in rhomboidal prisms, or in prisms of six sides.
michaelite: A subvariety of siliceous sinter, found in the isle of St. Michael.
michaelmas: The feast of St. Michael, a festival of the Romish church, celebrated Sept. 29; hence,
miche: To lie hid; to skulk; to retire or shrink from view.
micher: One who skulks, or creeps out of sight; a thief.
miching: Retiring; skulking; creeping from sight; mean; cowardly.
microcosm: Literally, the little world; but used for man, supposed to be an epitome of the universe or great world. Microcosmic salt, a triple salt of soda, ammonia and phosphoric acid, obtained from urine.
microcoustic: An instrument to augment small sounds, and assist in hearing.
micrography: The description of objects too small to be discerned without the aid of a microscope.
micrometer: An instrument for measuring small objects or spaces, by the help of which, the apparent magnitude of objects viewed through the microscope or telescope, is measured with great exactness.
microphone: An instrument to augment small sounds; a microcoustic.
microscope: An optical instrument consisting of lenses or mirrors, which magnify objects, and thus render visible minute objects which cannot be seen by the naked eye, or enlarge the apparent magnitude of small visible bodies, so as to enable us to examine their texture or construction.
microscopic: Made by the aid of a microscope; as microscopic observation.
Assisted by a microscope. Evading even the microscopic eye.
Resembling a microscope; capable of seeing small objects. Why has not man a microscopic eye?
Very small; visible only by the aid of a microscope; as a microscopic insect.
microscopical: Made by the aid of a microscope; as microscopic observation.
Assisted by a microscope. Evading even the microscopic eye.
Resembling a microscope; capable of seeing small objects. Why has not man a microscopic eye?
Very small; visible only by the aid of a microscope; as a microscopic insect.
microscopically: By the microscope; with minute inspection.
micturition: The act of making water, or passing the urine.
mid: Middle; at equal distance from extremes; as the mid hour of night.
Intervening. No more the mounting larks, while Daphne sings, Shall, lifting in mid air, suspend their wings.
mid-age: The middle of life, or persons of that age.
mid-course: The middle of the course or way.
mid-day: Being at noon; meridional; as the mid-day sun.
The middle of the day; noon.
middest: of mid. Among the middest crowd.
middle: Equally distant from the extremes; as the middle point of a line or circle; the middle station of life. The middle path or course is most safe.

Intermediate; intervening. Will, seeking good, finds many middle ends. Middle ages, the ages or period of time about equally distant from the decline of the Roman empire and the revival of letters in Europe, or from the eighth to the fifteenth century of the christian era.
The point or part equally distant from the extremities. See, there come people down by the middle of the land. Judges 9:37.
middle-aged: Being about the middle of the ordinary age of man. A middle-aged man is so called from the age of thirty five or forty to forty five or fifty.
middlemost: Being in the middle, or nearest the middle of a number of things that are near the middle. If a thing is in the middle, it cannot be more so, and in this sense the word is improper. But when two or more things are near the middle, one may be nearer than another.
middling: Of middle rank, state, size or quality; about equally distant from the extremes; moderate. Thus we speak of people of the middling class or sort, neither high nor low; of a man of middling capacity or understanding; a man of middling size; fruit of a middling quality.
mid-heaven: The middle of the sky or heaven.
midland: Being in the interior country; distant from the coast or sea shore; as midland towns or inhabitants.
Surrounded by the sea; mediterranean. And on the midland sea the French had aw’d.
midnight: The middle of the night; twelve o’clock at night.
Being in the middle of the night; as midnight studies.
Dark as midnight; very dark; as midnight gloom.
midriff: In anatomy, the diaphragm; the muscle which divides the trunk into two cavities, the thorax and abdomen.
midship: Being or belonging to the middle of a ship; as a midship beam.
midshipman: In ships of war, a kind of naval cadet, whose business is to second the orders of the superior officers and assist in the necessary business of the ship, particularly in managing the sails, that he may be trained to a knowledge of the machinery, discipline and operations of ships of war, and qualified for naval service.
midships: In the middle of a ship; properly amidships.
midst: The middle. There is nothing said or done in the midst of the play, which might not have been placed in the beginning. The phrase, in the midst, often signifies involved in, surrounded or overwhelmed by, or in the thickest part, or in the depths of; as in the midst of afflictions, troubles or cares; in the midst of our contemplations; in the midst of the battle; in the midst of pagan darkness and error; in the midst of gospel light; in the midst of the ocean; in the midst of civil dissensions. From the midst, from the middle, or from among. Deuteronomy 18:15.
In the middle. On earth, join all ye creatures to extol . Him first, Him last, Him midst, and without end.
midsummer: The middle of summer; the summer solstice, about the 21st of June.
midway: The middle of the way or distance. Paths indirect, or in the midway faint.
Being in the middle of the way or distance; as the midway air.
In the middle of the way or distance; half way. She met his glance midway.
midwife: A woman that assists other women in childbirth.
To perform the office of midwife.
midwifery: The art or practice of assisting women in childbirth; obstetrics.
mid-winter: The middle of winter, or the winter solstice, December 21. As the severity of winter in North America falls in January and February, the word ordinarily denotes this period, or some weeks after the winter solstice.
miemite: Granular miemite is a sub-variety of magnesian limestone, first found at Miemo, in Tuscany. It occurs massive, or crystallized in flat, double, three-sided pyramids. Its color is light green or greenish white.
mien: Look; air; manner; external appearance; carriage; as a lofty mien; a majestic mien.
might: of may. Had power or liberty. He might go, or might have gone.
It sometimes denotes was possible, implying ignorance of the fact in the speaker. Orders might have been given for the purpose.
Strength; force; power; primarily and chiefly, bodily strength or physical power; as, to work or strive with all one’s might. There small be no might in thy hand. Deuteronomy 28:32.
Political power or great achievements. The acts of David--with all his reign and his might. 7 Chronicles 29:30; 1 Kings 15:23.
National strength; physical power or military force. We have no might against this great company that cometh against us. 2 Chronicles 20:12.
mightily: With great power, force of strength; vigorously; as, to strive mightily.
Vehemently; with great earnestness. Cry mightily to God. Jonah 3:8.
Powerfully; with great energy. Whereto I also labor, striving according to his working, which worketh in me mightily. CoIossians 1:29.
With great strength of argument. He mightily convinced the Jews. Acts 18:28.
With great or irresistible force; greatly; extensively. So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed. Acts 19:20.
mightiness: Power; greatness; highth of dignity. How soon this mightiness meets misery!
A title of dignity; as their High Mightinesses.
mighty: Having great bodily strength or physical power; very strong or vigorous; as a mighty arm.
Very strong; valiant; bold; as a mighty man of valor. Judges 6:12.
Very powerful; having great command. Cush begat Nimrod; he began to be a mighty one on the earth. Genesis 10:8.
Very strong in numbers; as a mighty nation. Genesis 18:18.
Very strong or great in corporeal power; very able. Wo to them that are mighty to drink wine. Isaiah 5:22.
mignonette: An annual flower or plant of the genus Reseda, having the scent of raspberries.
migonet: An annual flower or plant of the genus Reseda, having the scent of raspberries.
migrate: To pass or remove from one country or from one state to another, with a view to permanent residence, or residence of some continuance. The first settlers of New England migrated first to Holland, and afterwards to America. Some species of fowls migrate in autumn to a warmer climate for a temporary residence. To change residence in the same city or state is not to migrate.
To pass or remove from one region or district to another for a temporary residence; as, the Tartars migrate for the sake of finding pasturage.
migrating: Removing from one state to another for a permanent residence. The people of the eastern states are continually migrating to the western states.
migration: The act of removing from one kingdom or state to another, for the purpose of permanent residence, or a residence of some continuance.
Change of place; removal; as the migration of the center of gravity.
migratory: Removing or accustomed to remove from one state or country to another for permanent residence.
Roving; wandering; occasionally removing for pasturage; as the migratory Tartars.
Passing from one climate to another; as fowls.
milch: Giving milk; as a milch cow. It is now applied only to beasts.
mild: Soft; gently and pleasantly affecting the senses; not violent; as a mild air; a mild sun; a mild temperature; a mild light. The rosy morn resigns her light . And milder glory to the noon. And with a milder gleam refreshed the sight.
Not acrid, pungent, corrosive or drastic; operating gently; not acrimonious; demulcent; mollifying; lenitive; assuasive; as a mild liquor; a mild cataplasm; a mild cathartic or emetic.
Tender and gentle in temper or disposition; kind; compassionate; merciful; clement; indulgent; not severe or cruel. It teaches us to adore him as a mild and merciful Being.
Not fierce, rough or angry; as mild words.
Placid; not fierce; not stern; not frowning; as a mild look or aspect.
mildew: Honey dew; a thick, clammy, sweet juice, found on the leaves of plants, which is said to injure the plants by corroding them, or otherwise preventing them from coming to perfection.
Spots on cloth or paper caused by moisture.
mildly: Softly; gently; tenderly; not roughly or violently; moderately; as, to speak mildly; to burn mildly; to operate mildly.
mildness: Softness; gentleness; as the mildness of words or speech; mildness of voice.
Tenderness; mercy; clemency; as mildness of temper.
Gentleness of operation; as the mildness of a medicine.
Softness; the quality that affects the senses pleasantly; as the mildness of fruit or of liquors.
Temperateness; moderate state; as the mildness of weather.
mile: A measure of length or distance, containing eight furlongs, 320 rods, poles or perches, yards, 5280 feet, or 80 chains. The Roman mile was a thousand paces, equal to yards English measure.
mileage: Fees paid for travel by the mile.
milestone: A stone set to mark the distance or space of a mile.
milfoil: A plant of the genus Achillea; yarrow.
miliary: Resembling millet seeds; as a miliary eruption; miliary glands. The miliary glands are the sebaceous glands of the skin.
Accompanied with an eruption like millet seeds; as a miliary fever.
milice: for militia, is not in use.
miliolite: Fossil remains of the Miliola, a genus of univalve shells.
militant: Fighting; combating; serving as a soldier.
The church militant, is the christian church on earth, which is supposed to be engaged in a constant warfare against its enemies; thus distinguished from the church triumphant, or in heaven.
military: Pertaining to soldiers or to arms; as a military parade or appearance; military discipline.
Engaged in the service of soldiers or arms; as a military man.
Warlike; becoming a soldier; as military virtue; military bravery.
Derived from the services or exploits of a soldier; as military renown.
Conformable to the customs or rules of armies or militia. The conduct of the officer was not military.
militate: To militate against, is to oppose; to be or to act in opposition. Paley writes, to militate with; but in America, against is generally used.
militia: The body of soldiers in a state enrolled for discipline, but not engaged in actual service except in emergencies; as distinguished from regular troops, whose sole occupation is war or military service. The militia of a country are the able bodied men organized into companies, regiments and brigades, with officers of all grades, and required by law to attend military exercises on certain days only, but at other times left to pursue their usual occupations.
milk: A white fluid or liquor, secreted by certain glands in female animals, and drawn from the breasts for the nourishment of their young.
The white juice of certain plants.
To draw or press milk from the breasts by the hand, as, to milk a cow.
milk-fever: A fever which accompanies the first flowing of milk in females after childbirth.
milk-hedge: A shrub growing on the Coromandel coast, containing a milky juice.
milkiness: Qualities like those of milk; softness.
milkmaid: A woman that milks or is employed in the dairy.
milkman: A man that sells milk or carries milk to market.
milkpail: A pail which receives the milk drawn from cows.
milkpan: A pan in which milk is set.
milkporridge: A species of food composed of milk or milk and water, boiled with meal or flour.
milkpottage: A species of food composed of milk or milk and water, boiled with meal or flour.
milkscore: An account of milk sold or purchased in small quantities, scored or marked.
milk-thistle: A plant of the genus Carduus.
milktooth: The fore tooth of a foal, which is cast within two or three years.
milk-vetch: A plant of the genus Astragalus.
milk-wort: A plant of the genus Euphorbia; spurge.
milky: Resembling milk; as milky sap or juice.
Soft; mild; gentle; timorous; as a milky heart.
milky-way: The galaxy; a broad luminous path or circle in the heavens, supposed to be the blended light of innumerable fixed stars, which are not distinguishable with ordinary telescopes.
mill: A money of account of the United States, value the tenth of a cent, or the thousandth of a dollar.

A complicated engine or machine for grinding and reducing to fine particles, grain, fruit or other substance, or for performing other operations by means of wheels and a circular motion; as a grist-mill for grain; a coffee-mill; a cider-mill; a bark-mill. The original purpose of mills was to comminute grain for food, but the word mill is now extended to engines or machines moved by water, wind or steam, for carrying on many other operations. We have oil-mills, saw-mills, slitting-mills, bark-mills, fulling-mills.
The house or building that contains the machinery for grinding.
To grind; to comminute; to reduce to fine particles or to small pieces.
millcog: The cog of a mill wheel.
milldam: A dam or mound to obstruct a water course, and raise the water to an altitude sufficient to turn a mill wheel.
millhorse: A horse that turns a mill.
millpond: A pond or reservoir of water raised for driving a mill wheel.
millrace: The current of water that drives a mill wheel, or the canal in which it is conveyed.
mill-sixpence: An old English coin first milled in 1561.
millstone: A stone used for grinding grain.
millenarian: Consisting of a thousand years; pertaining to the millenium.
A chiliast; one who believes in the millenium, and that Christ will reign on earth with his saints a thousand years before the end of the world.
millenial: Pertaining to the millenium, or to a thousand years; as millenial period; millenial happiness.
millenist: One who holds to the millenium.
millenium: A thousand years; a word used to denote the thousand years mentioned in Revelation 20:2-7. during which period Satan shall be bound and restrained from seducing men to sin, and Christ shall reign on earth with his saints.
milleped: The wood-louse, an insect having many feet, a species of Oniscus.
millepore: A genus of lithophytes or polypiers of various forms, which have the surface perforated with little holes or pores, or even without any apparent perforation.
miller: One whose occupation is to attend a grist-mill.
An insect whose wings appear as if covered with white dust or powder, like a miller’s clothes.
millesimal: Thousandth; consisting of thousandth parts; as millesimal fractions.
millet: A plant of the genus Milium, of several species, one of which is cultivated as an esculent grain. The Indian millet is of the genus Holcus.
milliary: Pertaining to a mile; denoting a mile; as a milliary column.
milligram: In the system of French weights and measures, the thousandth part of a gram, equal to a cubic millimeter of water. The milligram is equal to .0154 English grains.
milliliter: A French measure of capacity containing the thousandth part of a liter or cubic decimeter, equal to .06103 decimals of a cubic inch.
millimeter: A French lineal measure containing the thousandth part of a meter; equal to .03937 decimals of an inch. It is the least measure of length.
milliner: A woman who makes and sells head-dresses, hats or bonnetsfor females.
millinery: The articles made or sold by milliners, as head-dresses, hats or bonnets, laces, ribbons and the like.
million: The number of ten hundred thousand, or a thousand thousand. It is used as a noun or an adjective; as a million of men, or a million men. As a noun, it has a regular plural.
In common usage, a very great number. There are millions of truths that men are not concerned to know.
millionary: Pertaining to millions; consisting of millions; as the millionary chronology of the Pundits.
millrea: A coin of Portugal of the value of $1.24 cents.
millree: A coin of Portugal of the value of $1.24 cents.
milt: In anatomy, the spleen, a viscus situated in the left hypochondrium under the diaphragm.
The soft roe of fishes, or the spermatic part of the males.
To impregnate the roe or spawn of the female fish.
miltwort: A plant of the genus Asplenium.
mime: To mimic, or play the buffoon.
mimesis: In rhetoric, imitation of the voice or gestures of another.
mimetic: Apt to imitate; given to aping or mimicry.
mimic: One who imitates or mimics; a buffoon who attempts to excite laughter or derision by acting or speaking in the manner of another.
Amean or servile imitator. Of France the mimic, and of Spain the prey.
mimical: Imitative; inclined to imitate or to ape; having the practice or habit of imitating. Man is of all creatures the most mimical in gestures, speech.
Consisting of imitation; as mimic gestures. Mimic implies often something droll or ludicrous, or less dignified than imitative.
mimick: To imitate or ape for sport; to attempt to excite laughter or derision by acting or speaking like another; to ridicule by imitation. --The walk, the words, the gesture, could supply, The habit mimick, and the mien belie.
mimicry: Ludicrous imitation for sport or ridicule.
mina: A weight or denomination of money. The mina of the Old Testament was valued at sixty shekels. The Greek or Attic mina, was valued at a hundred drachmas, about f2. 17s. sterling, $10.44 cents.
minaret: A small spire or steeple, or spire-like ornament in Saracen architecture.
mince: To cut or chop into very small pieces; as, to mince meat.

To diminish in speaking; to retrench, cut off or omit a part for the purpose of suppressing the’ truth; to extenuate in representation. I know no way to mince it in love, but to say directly, I love you. Siren, now mince the sin, And mollify damnation with a phrase-- . If, to mince his meaning, I had either omitted some part of what he said, or taken from the strength of his expression, I certainly had wronged him. These--were forced to mince the matter.
To speak with affected softness; to clip words; not to utter the full sound.
To walk with short or diminished steps.
minced: Cut or chopped into very small pieces.
mince-pie: A pie made with minced meat and other ingredients, baked in paste.
minced-pie: A pie made with minced meat and other ingredients, baked in paste.
mincing: Cutting into small pieces; speaking or walking affectedly.
mind: Intention; purpose; design. The sacrifice of the wicked is abomination; how much more, when he bringeth it with a wicked mind. Proverbs 21:27.

Inclination; will; desire; a sense much used, but expressing less than settled purpose; as in the common phrases, “I wish to know your mind;” “let me know your mind;” “he had a mind to go;” “he has a partner to his mind.” .
Opinion; as, to express one’s mind. We are of one mind.
Memory; remembrance; as, to put one in mind; to call to mind; the fact is out of my mind; time out of mind. From the operations of the intellect in man, this word came to signify.

The intellectual or intelligent power in man; the understanding; the power that conceives, judges or reasons. I fear I am not in my perfect mind. So we speak of a sound mind, a disordered mind, a weak mind, a strong mind, with reference to the active powers of the understanding; and in a passive sense, it denotes capacity, as when we say, the mind cannot comprehend a subject.
minded: Disposed; inclined. If men were minded to live virtuously. Joseph was minded to put her away privily. Matthew 1:79. Minded is much used in composition; as high-minded; low-minded; feeble-minded; sober-minded; double-minded.
mindedness: Disposition; inclination towards any thing; as heavenly mindedness.
mindful: Attentive; regarding with care; bearing in mind; heedful; observant. I promise to be mindful of your admonitions. What is man, that thou art mindful of him? Psalm 8:4.
mindless: Inattentive; heedless; forgetful; negligent; careless. Cursed Athens, mindless of thy worth.
Not endued with mind or intellectual powers; as mindless bodies.
Stupid; unthinking; as a mindless slave.
mine: called sometimes a pronominal adj. My; belonging to me. It was formerly used before nouns beginning with vowels. “I kept myself from mine iniquity.” Psalm 78:23. But this use is no longer retained. We now use my before a vowel as well as before an articulation; as my iniquity. In present usage, my always precedes the noun, and mine follows the noun, and usually the verb; as, this is my book; this book is mine; it is called my book; the book is called mine: it is acknowledged to be mine. Mine sometimes supplies the place of a noun. Your sword and mine are different in construction.
A pit or excavation in the earth, from which metallic ores, mineral substances and other fossil bodies are taken by digging. The pits from which stones only are taken, are called quarries.
In the military art, a subterraneous canal or passage dug under the wall or rampart of a fortification, where a quantity of power may be lodged for blowing up the works.
A rich source of wealth or other good.
To dig a mine or pit in the earth.
miner: One that digs for metals and other fossils.
One who digs canals or passages under the walls of a fortArmies have sappers and miners.
mineral: A body destitute of organization, and which naturally exists within the earth or at its surface. Minerals were formerly divided into salts, earths, inflammables and ores; a division which serves for a general distribution, but a more scientific arrangement into classes, orders, genera, species, subspecies and varieties, has been adopted to meet the more precise views of modern mineralogists.
Pertaining to minerals; consisting of fossil substances; as the mineral kingdom.
Impregnated with minerals or fossil matter; as mineral waters; a mineral spring.
mineralist: One versed or employed in minerals.
mineralization: The process of forming an ore by combination with another substance; the natural operation of uniting a metallic substance with another.
The process of converting into a mineral, as a bone or a plant.
The act of impregnating with a mineral, as water.
mineralize: In mineralogy, to combine with a metal in forming an ore or mineral. Sulphur mineralizes many of the metals.
To convert into a mineral. In these caverns, the bones are not mineralized.
To impregnate with a mineral substance; as, to mineralize water.
mineralized: Deprived of its usual properties by being combined with another substance or formed into an ore; as, metallic substances are mineralized.
mineralizer: A substance which mineralizes another or combines with it in an ore, and thus deprives it of its usual and peculiar properties. Sulphur is one of the most common mineralizers.
mineralogical: Pertaining to the science of minerals; as a mineralogical table.
mineralogist: One who is versed in the science of minerals, or one who treats or discourses of the properties of mineral bodies.
mineralogy: The science which treats of the properties of mineral substances, and teaches us to characterize, distinguish and class them according to their properties. It comprehends the study or science of all inorganic substances in the earth or on its surface.
mingle: To mix; to blend; to unite in one body; as, to mingle liquors of different kinds.
To mix or blend without order or promiscuously. There was fire mingled with hail. Exodus 9:24.
To compound; to unite in a mass, as solid substances; as, to mingle flour, sugar and eggs in cookery.
To join in mutual intercourse or in society. The holy seed have mingled themselves with the people of those lands. Ezra 9:2; Psalm 106:35.
To contaminate; to render impure; to debase by mixture. The best of us appear contented with a mingled imperfect virtue.
miniardize: To render soft, delicate or dainty.
miniate: é. To paint or tinge with vermillion.
miniature: A painting in water colors on vellum, ivory or paper, with points or dots; sometimes in oil colors. The term is usually applied to portraits painted on a very small scale.
A picture or representation in a small compass, or less than the reality.
minikin: Small; diminutive; used in slight contempt.
minim: A little man or being; a dwarf.
One of a certain reformed order of Franciscans or Minimi .
A note in music, equal to half a semi-breve or two crotchets.
minimum: The least quantity assignable in a given case.
minimus: A being of the smallest size.
mining: Digging into the earth, as for fossils and minerals; sapping. 1.a. Designating the business of digging mines; as the mining districts of Siberia.
minion: min’yon. A favorite; a darling; particularly, the favorite of a prince, on whom he lavishes his favors; one who gains favors by flattery or mean adulation. Edward sent an army into Ireland, not for conquest, but to guard the person of his minion, Piers Gaviston. The drowsy tyrant by his minions led.
A small kind of printing types.
minious: Of the color of red lead or vermillion.
minister: Properly, a chief servant; hence, an agent appointed to transact or manage business under the authority of another; in which sense, it is a word of very extensive application. Moses rose up and his minister Joshua. Exodus 24:13.

One to whom a king or prince entrusts the direction of affairs of state; as minister of state; the prime minister. In modern governments, the secretaries or heads of the several departments or branches of government are the ministers of the chief magistrate.
A magistrate; an executive officer. For he is the minister of God to thee for good. Romans 13:4.
A delegate; an embassador; the representative of a sovereign at a foreign court; usually such as is resident at a foreign court, but not restricted to such.
One who serves at the altar; one who performs sacerdotal duties; the pastor of a church, duly authorized or licensed to preach the gospel and administer the sacraments. Ephesians 3:7.
ministerial: Attending for service; attendant; acting at command. Enlight’ning spirits and ministerial flames.
Acting under superior authority; pertaining to a minister. For the ministerial offices in court, there must be an eye to them.
Pertaining to executive offices, as distinct from judicial. The office and acts of a sheriff are ministerial.

Sacerdotal; pertaining to ministers of the gospel; as ministerial garments; ministerial duties. Genuine ministerial prudence keeps back no important truth, listens to no compromise with sin, connives at no fashionable vice, cringes before no lordly worldling.
Pertaining to ministers of state; as ministerial circles; ministerial benches.
ministerially: In a ministerial manner or character.
ministering: Attending and serving as a subordinate agent; serving under superior authority. Hebrews 1:14.
Affording aid or supplies; administering things needful.
ministrant: Performing service as a minister; attendant on service; acting under command. Princedoms and dominations ministrant.
ministration: The act of performing service as a subordinate agent; agency; intervention for aid or service. --Because their widows were neglected in the daily ministrations. Acts 6:1.
Office of a minister; service; ecclesiastical function. As soon as the days of his ministration were ended. Luke 1:23.
ministry: The office, duties or functions of a subordinate agent of any kind.
Agency; service; aid; interposition; instrumentality. He directs the affairs of this world by the ordinary ministry of second causes.

Ecclesiastical function; agency or service of a minister of the gospel or clergyman in the modern church, or of priests, apostles and evangelists in the ancient. Acts 1:25; Romans 12:7; 2 Timothy 4:5; Numbers 4:47.
Time of ministration; duration of the office of a minister, civil or ecclesiastical. The war with France was during the ministry of Pitt .
Persons who compose the executive government or the council of a supreme magistrate; the body of ministers of state.W// .
ministryship: for ministry, is little used and hardly proper.
minium: The red oxyd of lead, produced by calcination. Lead exposed to air while melting is covered with a gray dusky pellicle. This taken off and agitated becomes a greenish gray powder, inclining to yellow. This oxyd, separated by sifting from the grains of lead which it contains, and exposed to a more intense heat, takes a deep yellow color, and in this state it is called massicot. The latter, slowly heated, takes a beautiful red color, and is called minium.
mink: An American quadruped of the genus Mustela, an amphibious animal that burrows in the earth on the side of a river or pond, whose fur is more valuable than that of the muskrat.
minnoc: used by Shakespeare, is supposed by Johnson to be the same as minx.
minnow: A very small fish, a species of Cyprinus.
minow: A very small fish, a species of Cyprinus.
minor: Less; smaller; sometimes applied to the bulk or magnitude of a single object; more generally to amount, degree or importance. We say, the minor divisions of a body, the minor part of a body; opposed to the major part. We say, minor sums, minor faults, minor considerations, details or arguments. In the latter phrases, minor is equivalent to small, petty, inconsiderable, not principal, important or weighty.
In music, less or lower by a lesser semi-tone; as a third minor. Asia Minor, the Lesser Asia, that part of Asia which lies between the Euxine on the north, and the Mediterranean on the south.

A person of either sex under age; one who is under the authority of his parents or guardians, or who is not permitted by law to make contracts and manage his own property. By the laws of Great Britain and of the United States, persons are minors till they are twenty one years of age.

In logic, the second proposition of a regular syllogism, as in the following: . Every act of injustice partakes of meanness. To take money from another by gaming, or reputation by seduction, are acts of injustice. Therefore the taking of money from another by gaming, or reputation by seduction, partake of meanness.
minority: The state of being under age.

The smaller number; as the minority of the senate or house of representatives; opposed to majority. We say, the minority was large or small; AB was in the minority; the minority must be ruled by the majority.
minotaur: A fabled monster, half man and half bull.
minster: A monastery; an ecclesiastical convent or fraternity; but it is said originally to have been the church of a monastery; a cathedral church.
minstrel: A singer and musical performer on instruments. Minstrels were formerly poets as well as musicians, and held in high repute by our rude ancestors. Their attendance was sought and their performances lavishly rewarded by princes. It was in the character of a minstrel that king Alfred entered the camp of the Danes his enemies, and explored their situation.
minstrelsy: The arts and occupations of minstrels; instrumental music.
Anumber of musicians. The minstrelsy of heaven.
mint: The place where money is coined by public authority. In Great Britain, formerly, there was a mint in almost every county; but the privilege of coining is now considered as a royal prerogative in that country, and as the prerogative of the sovereign power in other countries. The only mint now in Great Britain is in the Tower of London. The mint in the United States is in Philadelphia .
A place of invention or fabrication; as a mint of phrases; a mint of calumny.
To coin; to make and stamp money.
To invent; to forge; to fabricate.
mintage: That which is coined or stamped.
mintman: A coiner; one skilled in coining or in coins.
mintmaster: The master or superintendent of a mint.
minuend: In arithmetic, the number form which another number is to subtracted.
minuet: A slow graceful dance, consisting of a coupee, a high step and a balance.
A tune or air to regulate the movements in the dance so called; a movement of three crotchets or three quavers in a bar.
minum: A small kind of printing types; now written minion.
A note of slow time containing two crotchets; now written minim, which see.
minute: Very small, little or slender; of very small bulk or size; small in consequence; as a minute grain of sand; a minute filament. The blood circulates through very minute vessels. Minute divisions of a subject often perplex the understanding. Minute details are tedious.
Attending to small things; critical; as minute observation.
A small portion of time or duration, being the sixtieth part of an hour. Since you are not sure of a minute, throw not away an hour.
In geometry, the sixtieth part of a degree of a circle.
In architecture, the sixtieth, but sometimes the thirtieth part of a module.
minute-glass: A glass, the sand of which measures a minute.
minute-hand: The hand that points to the minutes on a clock or watch.
minutely: To a small point of time, space or matter; exactly; nicely; as, to measure the length of any thing minutely; to ascertain time minutely; to relate a story minutely.
Every minute; with very little time intervening. As if it were minutely proclaimed in thunder from heaven.
minuteness: Extreme smallness, fineness or slenderness; as the minuteness of the particles of air or of a fluid; the minuteness of the filaments of cotton; the minuteness of details in narration.
Attention to small things; critical exactness; as the minuteness of observation or distinction.
minute-watch: A watch that distinguishes minutes of time, or on which minutes are marked.
miracle: Literally, a wonder or wonderful thing; but appropriately,

In theology, an event or effect contrary to the established constitution and course of things, or a deviation from the known laws of nature; a supernatural event. Miracles can be wrought only by Almighty power, as when Christ healed lepers, saying, “I will, be thou clean,” or calmed the tempest, “Peace, be still." . They considered not the miracle of the loaves. Mark 6:52. A man approved of God by miracles and signs. Acis 2:22.
Anciently, a spectacle or dramatic representation exhibiting the lives of the saints.
miracle-monger: An impostor who pretends to work miracles.
miraculous: Performed supernaturally, or by a power beyond the ordinary agency of natural laws; effected by the direct agency of Almighty power, and not by natural causes; as the miraculous healing of the sick or raising the dead by Christ.

Supernatural; furnished supernaturally, or competent to perform miracles; as the miraculous powers of the Apostles. Miraculous, applied to the extraordinary powers of the Apostles, may mean conferred by supernatural agency, or competent to work miracles. I believe it is generally used in the latter sense.
In a less definite sense, wonderful; extra-ordinary.
miraculously: By miracle; supernaturally. AEneas, wounded as he was, could not have engaged him in single combat, unless his hurt had been miraculously healed.
miraculousness: The state of being effected by miracle or by supernatural agency.
mirador: A balcony or gallery commanding an extensive view.
mire: Deep mud; earth so wet and soft as to yield to the feet and to wheels.
To plunge and fix in mire; to set or stall in mud. We say, a horse, an ox or a carriage is mired, when it has sunk deep into mud and its progress is stopped.
To soil or daub with mud or foul matter.
To sink in mud, or to sink so deep as to be unable to move forward.
mire-crow: The sea-crow or pewit gull, of the genus Larus.
miriness: The state of consisting of deep mud.
mirror: A looking glass; any glass or polished substance that forms images by the reflection of rays of light. In the clear mirror of thy ruling star . I saw, alas! some dread event depend.

A pattern; an exemplar; that on which men ought to fix their eyes; that which gives a true representation, or in which a true image may be seen. O goddess, heavenly bright, Mirror of grace and majesty divine.
mirth: Social merriment; hilarity; high excitement of pleasurable feelings in company; noisy gayety; jollity. Mirth differs from joy and cheerfulness, as always implying noise. With genial joy to warm the soul, Bright Helen mixed a mirth-inspiring bowl. I will cause to cease the voice of mirth from Judah and Jerusalem. Jeremiah 7:34.
mirthful: Merry; jovial; festive. The feast was served, the bowl was crown’d, To the king’s pleasure went the mirthful round.
miry: Abounding with deep mud; full of mire; as a miry road; a miry lane.
mis: a prefix, denotes error, or erroneous, wrong, from the verb miss, to err, togo wrong.
misacceptation: The act of taking or understanding in a wrong sense.
misadventure: Mischance; misfortune; ill luck; an unlucky accident.
In law, homicide by misadventure, is when a man, doing a lawful act, without any intention of injury, unfortunately kills another. This is called excusable homicide.
misanthropic: Hating or having a dislike to mankind.
misanthropical: Hating or having a dislike to mankind.
misanthropy: Hatred or dislike to mankind; opposed to philanthropy.
misapplication: A wrong application; an application to a wrong person or purpose.
misapplied: Applied to a wrong person or purpose.
misapply: To apply to a wrong person or purpose; as to misapply a name or title; to misapply our talents or exertions; to misapply public money.
misapplying: Applying to a wrong person or purpose.
misapprehend: To misunderstand; to take in a wrong sense.
misapprehension: A mistaking or mistake; wrong apprehension of one’s meaning or of a fact.
misbecome: misbecum’. Not to become; to suit ill; not to befit. Thy father will not act what misbecomes him.
misbecoming: or a. Unseemly; unsuitable; improper; indecorous.
misbegot: or a. Unlawfully or irregularly begotten.
misbegotten: or a. Unlawfully or irregularly begotten.
misbehave: To behave ill; to conduct one’s self improperly.
misbehaved: Guilty of ill behavior; ill bred; rude.
misbehavior: misbeha’vyor. Ill conduct; improper, rude or uncivil behavior.
misbeliever: One who believes wrongly; one who holds a false religion.
miscall: To call by a wrong name; to name improperly.
miscarriage: Unfortunate event of an undertaking; failure. When a counselor, to save himself, Would lay miscarriages upon his prince.
Ill conduct; evil or improper behavior; as the failings and miscarriages of the righteous.
Abortion; the act of bringing forth before the time.
miscarry: To fail of the intended effect; not to succeed; to be unsuccessful; to suffer defeat; applied to persons or undertakings, and to things. We say, a project, scheme, design, enterprise, attempt, has miscarried. Have you not heart of Frederick, the great soldier, who miscarried at sea? My ships have all miscarried.
To bring forth young before the proper time; to suffer abortion.
miscarrying: Failing of the intended effect; suffering abortion. Hosea 9:14.
miscast: é To cast or reckon erroneously.
miscellanarian: Belonging to miscellanies; of miscellanies. Miscellanarian authors.
miscellane: A mixture of two or more sorts of grain; now called mesilin.
miscellaneous: Mixed; mingled; consisting of several kinds; as a miscellaneous publication; a miscellaneous rabble.
miscellaneousness: The state of being mixed; composition of various kinds.
miscellany: A mass or mixture of various kinds; particularly,
A book or pamphlet containing a collection of compositions on various subjects, or a collection of various’ kinds’ of compositions.
mischance: III luck; ill fortune; misfortune; mishap; misadventure.
Ill luck; ill fortune; misfortune; mishap; misadventure. It is a man’s unhappiness, his mischance or calamity, but not his fault .
mischaracterize: To characterize falsely or erroneously; to give a wrong character to. They totally mischaracterize the action.
mischarge: To mistake in charging, as an account.
mischief: Harm; hurt; injury; damage; evil, whether intended or not. A new law is made to remedy the mischief.
Intentional injury; harm or damage done by design. Thy tongue deviseth mischief. Psalm 52:2.
Ill consequence; evil; vexatious affair. The mischief was, these allies would never allow that the common enemy was subdued.
To hurt; to harm; to injure.
mischief-maker: One who makes mischief; one who excites or instigates quarrels or enmity.
mischief-making: Causing harm; exciting enmity or quarrels.
mischievous: Harmful; hurtful; injurious; making mischief; of persons; as a mischievous man or disposition.
Hurtful; noxious; as a- mischievous thing.
Inclined to do harm; as a mischievous boy.
mischievously: With injury, hurt, loss or damage. We say, the law operates mischievously.
With evil intention or disposition. The injury was done mischievously.
mischievousness: Disposition to do harm, or to vex or annoy; as the mischievousness of youth. Mischief denotes injury, harm or damage of less malignity and magnitude than what are usually called crimes. We never give the name of mischief to theft, robbery or murder. And it so commonly implies intention in committing petty offenses, that it shocks us to hear the word applied to the calamities inflicted by Providence. We say, a tempest has done great damage, but not mischief. In like manner, the adjective mischievous is not applied to thieves, pirates and other felons, but to persons committing petty trespasses and offenses.
mischna: A part of the Jewish Talmud.
mischoose: mischooz’. To choose wrong; to make a wrong choice.
miscible: That may be mixed. Oil and water are not miscible.
misconceit: Erroneous conception; false opinion; wrong notion or understanding of a thing. Great errors and dangers result from a misconception of the names of things.
misconception: Erroneous conception; false opinion; wrong notion or understanding of a thing. Great errors and dangers result from a misconception of the names of things.
misconceive: or i. To receive a false notion or opinion of any thing; to misjudge; to have an erroneous understanding of any thing. To yield to others just and reasonable causes of those things, which, for want of due consideration heretofore, they have misconceived.
misconduct: Wrong conduct; ill behavior; ill management.
misconstruction: Wrong interpretation of words or things; a mistaking of the true meaning; as a misconstruction of words or actions.
misconstrue: To interpret erroneously either words or things. It is important not to misconstrue the Scriptures. Do not, great sir, misconstrue his intent. A virtuous emperor was much affected to find his actions misconstrued.
misconstruer: One who makes a wrong interpretation.
miscorrect: To correct erroneously; to mistake in attempting to correct another. He passed the first seven years of his life at Mantua, not seventeen, as Scaliger miscorrects his author.
miscorrected: Mistaken in the attempt to correct.
miscount: To count erroneously; to mistake in counting.
miscreance: Unbelief; false faith; adherence to a false religion.
miscreancy: Unbelief; false faith; adherence to a false religion.
miscreant: An infidel, or one who embraces a false faith.
A vile wretch; an unprincipled fellow.
misdeed: An evil deed; a wicked action. Evils which our own misdeeds have wrought.
misdeem: To judge erroneously; to misjudge; to mistake in judging.
misdemeanor: Ill behavior; evil conduct; fault; mismanagement.

In law, an offense of a less atrocious nature than a crime. Crimes and misdemeanors are mere synonymous terms; but in common usage, the word crime is made to denote offenses of a deeper and more atrocious dye, while small faults and omissions of less consequence are comprised under the gentler name of misdemeanors.
misdirect: To give a wrong direction to; as to misdirect a passenger.
To direct to a wrong person or place; as, to misdirect a letter.
misdirected: Directed wrong, or to a wrong person or place.
misdirecting: Directing wrong, or to a wrong person or place.
misdo: To do wrong; to do amiss; to commit a crime or fault.
misdoer: One who does wrong; one who commits a fault or crime.
misdoing: Doing wrong; committing a fault or crime.
A wrong done; a fault or crime; an offense.
misdoubt: misdout’. To suspect of deceit or danger.
mise: In law, an issue to be tried at the grand assize.
A tax or tallage; in Wales, an honorary gift of the people to a new king or prince of Wales; also, a tribute paid in the county Palatine of Chester at the change of the owner of the earldoms.
misemploy: To employ to no purpose, or to a bad purpose; as, to misemploy time, power, advantages, talents
misemployed: Used to no purpose, or to a bad one.
misemploying: Using to no purpose, or to a bad one.
misemployment: Ill employment; application to no purpose, or to a bad purpose.
misentry: An erroneous entry or charge, as of an account.
miser: s as z. A miserable person; one wretched or afflicted.
An extremely covetous person; a sordid wretch; a niggard; one who in wealth makes himself miserable by the fear of poverty. No silver saints by dying misers given.
miserabale: Very unhappy from grief, pain, calamity, poverty, apprehension of evil, or other cause. It however expresses somewhat less than wretched. What hopes delude thee, miserable man?
Very poor; worthless. Miserable comforters are ye all. Job 16:2.
Causing unhappiness or misery. What’s more miserable than discontent?
Very poor or mean; as a miserable hut; miserable clothing.
miserably: Unhappily; calamitously. The fifth was miserably stabbed to death.
Very poorly or meanly; wretchedly. They were miserably entertained.
misery: Great unhappiness; extreme pain of body or mind. A man suffers misery from the gout, or from great afflictions, distress, calamity, and other evils. Misery expresses somewhat less than wretchedness. Misery is as really the fruit of vice reigning in the heart, as tares are the produce of tares sown in the field.
Calamity; misfortune; natural evils which are the cause of misery. And mourn the miseries of human life.
misfall: To befall, as ill luck; to happen to unluckily.
misfeasance: misfe’zance. In law, a trespass; a wrong done.
misform: To make of an ill form; to put in an ill shape.
misfortune: Ill fortune; ill luck; calamity; an evil or cross accident; as loss of property at sea or by fire. Consider why the change was wrought, You'll find it his misfortune, not his fault.
misgive: misgiv’. To fill with doubt; to deprive of confidence; to fail; usually applied to the heart. So doth my heart misgive me. His heart misgave him.
misgiving: Filling with doubt or distrust; failing.
A failing of confidence; doubt; distrust. Doubts, suspicions and misgivings.
misgovern: To govern ill; to administer unfaithfully. Solyman charged him bitterly that he had misgoverned the state.
misgoverned: Rude; unrestrained; as rude, misgoverned hands.
misguide: To lead or guide into error; to direct ill; as, to misguide the understanding or mind.
misguided: Let astray by evil counsel or wrong direction; as a misguided prince.
misguiding: Giving wrong direction to; leading into error.
misgum: An anguilliform fish about the size of a common eel.
misgurn: An anguilliform fish about the size of a common eel.
mishap: III chance; evil accident; ill luck; misfortune.
Ill chance; evil accident; ill luck; misfortune. Secure from worldly chances and mishaps.
mishna: A collection or digest of Jewish traditions and explanations of Scripture.
mishnic: Pertaining or relating to the Mishna.
misimprove: misimproov’. To improve to a bad purpose; to abuse; as, to misimprove time, talents
misimprovement: misimproov’ment. Ill use or employment; improvement to a bad purpose.
misinform: To give erroneous information to; to communicate an incorrect statement of facts.
misinformation: Wrong informations; false account or intelligence received.
misinterpret: To interpret erroneously; to understand or to explain in a wrong sense.
misjoin: é To join unfitly or improperly.
misjudge: misjudg’. To mistake in judging of; to judge erroneously.
misjudg’. To err in judgment; to form false opinions or notions.
misjudging: Judging erroneously of; forming a wrong opinion or inference.
miskindle: To kindle amiss; to inflame to a bad purpose.
mislaid: Laid in a wrong place, or place not recollected; lost.
mislay: To lay in a wrong place. The fault is generally mislaid upon nature.
To lay in a place not recollected; to lose. If the butler be the tell-tale, mislay a spoon so as he may never find it.
mislayer: One that lays in a wrong place; one that loses.
mislaying: Laying in a wrong place, or place not remembered; losing.
misle: mis’. To rain in very fine drops, like a thick mist.
mislead: t pret. and pp. misled. To lead into a wrong way or path; to lead astray; to guide into error; to cause to mistake; to deceive. Trust not servants who mislead or misinform you, But of the two, less dangerous is th’ offense, To tire our patience, than mislead our sense.
misleading: Leading into error; causing to err; deceiving.
misled: of mislead. Let into error; led a wrong way. --To give due light . To the misled and lonely traveller.
mislike: To dislike; to disapprove; to have aversion to; as, to mislike a man or an Opinion.
mismanage: To manage ill; to administer improperly; as, to mismanage public affairs.
To behave ill; to conduct amiss.
mismanagment: Ill or improper management; ill conduct; as the mismanagement of public or private affairs.
mismark: To mark with the wrong token; to mark erroneously.
misname: To call by the wrong name.
misnomer: In law, the mistaking of the true name of a person; a misnaming.
misobserve: misobzerv’. To observe inaccurately; to mistake in observing.
misorder: To order ill; to manage erroneously.
To manage ill; to conduct badly.
mispersuade: To persuade amiss, or to lead to a wrong notion.
mispersuasion: A false persuasion; wrong notion or opinion.
mispikel: Arsenical pyrite; an ore of arsenic, containing this metal in combination with iron, sometimes found in cubic crystals, but more often without any regular form.
misplace: To put in a wrong place; as, the book is misplaced.
To place on an improper object; as, he misplaced his confidence.
misplaced: Put in a wrong place, or on an improper object.
misplacing: Putting in a wrong place, or on a wrong object.
mispoint: To point improperly; to err in punctuation.
misprint: ét. To mistake in printing; to print wrong.
A mistake in printing; a deviation from the copy.
misprise: To slight or undervalue. O for those vanish’d hours, so much mispris’d.
misprize: To slight or undervalue. O for those vanish’d hours, so much mispris’d.
misprision: In law, any high offense under the degree of capital, but nearly bordering thereon. Misprision is contained in every treason and felony. Misprisions are divided into negative and positive; negative, which consist in the concealment of something which ought to be revealed; and positive, which consist in the commission of something which ought not to be done. Misprision of treason, consists in a bare knowledge and concealment of treason, without assenting to it. Maladministration in offices of high public trust, is a positive misprision.
misprofess: To make a false profession; to make pretensions to skill which is not possessed.
mispronounce: mispronouns’. To pronounce erroneously; as, to mispronounce a word, a name
misproportion: To err in proportioning one thing to another; to join without due proportion.
misquotation: An erroneous quotation; the act of quoting wrong.
misquote: To quote erroneously; to cite incorrectly.
misrate: To rate erroneously; to estimate falsely.
misreckoning: Reckoning wrong; and as a noun, an erroneous computation.
misspending: Spending to no purpose, or to a bad one.
misspense: misspens’. A spending improperly; a wasting.
misspent: Ill spent; expended or consumed to no purpose, or to a bad one; as misspent time or life.
misstate: To state wrong; to make an erroneous representation of facts; as, to misstate a question in debate.
misstatement: A wrong statement; an erroneous representation, verbal or written; as a misstatement of facts in testimony, or of accounts in a report.
missy: The sulphate of iron, having lost the water of its crystallization, is called sori; more thoroughly calcined, it is yellow, and called missy.
mist: Water falling in very numerous, but fine and almost imperceptible drops. A mist is a multitude of small but solid globules, which therefore descend.
That which dims or darkens, and obscures or intercepts vision. His passion cast a mist before his sense.
é. To cloud; to cover with vapor.
mistakable: That may be misconceived or mistaken.
mistake: é. To take wrong; to conceive or understand erroneously; to misunderstand or misapprehend. ‘Tis to mistake them costs the time and pain.
To take one thing or person for another. We mistake the eloquence of self-apology for the animation of conscious integrity. A man may mistake the love of virtue for the practice of it.
To err in opinion or judgment. Servants mistake, and sometimes occasion misunderstanding among friends.
An error in opinion or judgment; misconception. Infallibility is an absolute security of the understanding from all possibility of mistake.
A slip; a fault; an error. There is a mistake in the account or in the date.
mistaken: In the use of this participle, there is a peculiarity which ought to be carefully noticed. When used of persons, it signified to be in an error, to be wrong; as, I am mistaken, you are mistaken, he is mistaken. But when used of things, it signified misunderstood, misconceived; as, the sense of the passage is mistaken, that is, not rightly understood.
mistaking: Making a mistake; erring from the truth; misconceiving.
mistaught: Wrongly taught; as a mistaught youth.
misteach: To teach wrong; to instruct erroneously.
mister: The common title of address to gentlemen, and to men of all classes. In writing, it is expressed by the abbreviation Mr.
misthought: of misthink. Thought wrong of. Adam, misthought of her to thee so dear.
mistime: To time wrong; not to adapt to the time.
mistimed: Ill timed; done at a wrong time.
mistiness: A state of being misty; a state of thick rain in very small drops.
mistitle: To call by a wrong title or name.
mistle: mis’l. To fall in very fine drops, as rain.
mistletoe: mis'Ito. A plant or shrub that grows on trees. It is of the genus Viscum. The berry contains a glutinous substance, and the shrub is said to be propagated by birds. This plant was held in great veneration by the Druids.
misletoe: mis'Ito. A plant or shrub that grows on trees. It is of the genus Viscum. The berry contains a glutinous substance, and the shrub is said to be propagated by birds. This plant was held in great veneration by the Druids.
mistress: A woman who governs; correlative to servant, slave, or subject. My mistress here lies murdered in her bed.
The female head of a family.
That which governs; a sovereign. Rome was mistress of the world.
One that commands, or has possession and sovereignty. The queen is mistress of the Indies.
A female who is well skilled in any thing; as, she is mistress of arithmetic.
mistrust: Want of confidence or trust; suspicion.
To suspect; to doubt; to regard with jealousy or suspicion. Fate her own book mistrusted at the sight.
mistune: é To tune wrong or erroneously; to put out of tune.
misty: Overspread with mist; filled with very minute drops of rain; as misty weather; a misty atmosphere; a misty night or day.
Dim; obscure; clouded; as misty sight.
misunderstand: To misconceive; to mistake; to take in a wrong sense.
misunderstanding: Misconception; mistake of the meaning; error.
Disagreement; difference; dissension; sometimes a softer name for quarrel.
misuse: To treat or use improperly; to use to a bad purpose.
misyu’se. Ill treatment; improper use; employment to a bad purpose; as the misuse of mercies.
misused: misyu’zed. Improperly used or applied; misapplied; misemployed; abused.
mite: A very small insect of the genus Acarus.
In Scripture, a small piece of money, the quarter of a denarius, or about seven English farthings.
Any thing proverbially very small; a very little particle or quantity.
The twentieth part of a grain.
miter: A sacerdotal ornament worn on the head by bishops and certain abbots, on solemn occasions.
In architecture, an angle of 45 degrees.
In Irish history, a sort of base money or coin.
Figuratively, the dignity of bishops or abbots.
mitered: Honored with the privilege of wearing a miter.
Cut or joined at an angle of 45 degrees.
mithridate: In pharmacy, an antidote against poison, or a composition in form of an electuary, supposed to serve either as a remedy or a preservative against poison. It takes its name from Mithridates, king of Pontus, the inventor.
mithridatic: Pertaining to mithridate, or its inventor, Mithridates.
mitigate: To alleviate, as suffering; to assuage; to lessen; as, to mitigate pain or grief. And counsel mitigates the greatest smart.
To make less severe; as, to mitigate doom.
To abate; to make less rigorous; to moderate; as, to mitigate cold; to mitigate the severity of the season.
To temper; to moderate; to soften in harshness or severity. We could wish that the rigor of their opinions were allayed and mitigated.
mitigation: Alleviation; abatement or diminution of any thing painful, harsh, severe, afflictive or calamitous; as the mitigation of pain, grief, rigor, severity, punishment or penalty.
mitten: A cover for the hand, worn to defend it from cold or other injury. It differs from a glove, in not having a separate cover for each finger.
A cover for the arm only. To handle without mittens, to treat roughly; a popular colloquial phrase.
mittimus: In law, a precept or command in writing, under the hand or hand and seal of a justice of the peace or other proper officer, directed to the keeper of a prison, requiring him to imprison an offender; a warrant of commitment to prison.
A writ for removing records from one court to another.
mitu: A fowl of the turkey kind, found in Brazil.
mix: é pret. and pp. mixed or mixt.
To unite or blend promiscuously two or more ingredients into a mass or compound; applied both to solids and liquids; as, to mix flour and salt; to mix wines.
To join; to associate; to unite with in company. Ephraim, he hath mixed himself among the people. Hosea 7:8.
To join; to mingle. x. You mix your sadness with some fear.
To unite with a crowd or multitude.
mixed: United in a promiscuous mass or compound; blended; joined; mingled; associated.
a. Promiscuous; consisting of various kinds or different things; as a mixed multitude.
mixing: Uniting or blending in a mass or compound; joining in company; associating.
mixtilineal: Containing a mixture of lines, right, curved.
mixtilinear: Containing a mixture of lines, right, curved.
mixture: The act of mixing, or state of being mixed. Compounds are made by the mixture of different substances.
A mass or compound, consisting of different ingredients blended without order. In this life there is a mixture of good and evil. Most wines in market are base mixtures.
The ingredient added and mixed. Cicero doubted whether it is possible for a community to exist without a prevailing mixture of piety in its constitution.
In pharmacy, a liquid medicine which receives into its composition not only extracts, salts and other substances dissolvable in water, but earths, powders and other substances not dissolvable.

In chimistry, mixture differs from combination. In mixture, the several ingredients are blended without an alteration of the substances, each of which still retains its own nature and properties. In combination, the substances unite by chimical attraction, and losing their distinct properties, they form a compound differing in its properties from either of the ingredients.
mizmaze: A cant word for a maze or labyrinth.
mizzen: miz’n. In sea-language, the aftermost of the fixed sails of a ship, extended sometimes by a gaff, and sometimes by a yard which crosses the mast obliquely.
mizzen-mast: The mast which supports the after-sails, and stands nearest to the stern.
mnemonics: The art of memory; the precepts and rules intended to teach the method of assisting the memory.
moan: To lament; to deplore; to bewail with an audible voice. Ye floods, ye woods, ye echoes, moan . My dear Columbo dead and gone.
To grieve; to make lamentations. Unpitied and unheard, where misery moans.
Lamentation; audible expression of sorrow; grief expressed in words or cried. Sullen moans, Hollow groans.
moat: In fortification, a ditch or deep trench round the rampart of a castle or other fortified place. It is sometimes filled with water.
To surround with a ditch for defense; as a moated castle.
mob: A crowd or promiscuous multitude of people, rude, tumultuous and disorderly.
A disorderly assembly. Had every Athenian citizen been a Socrates, every Athenian assembly would still have been a mob.
To attack in a disorderly crowd; to harass tumultuously.
To wrap up in a cowl or vail.
mobbish: Like a mob; tumultuous; mean; vulgar.
mobcap: A plain cap or head-dress for females.
mobile: The mob; the populace. Primum mobile, in the ancient astronomy, a ninth heaven or sphere, supposed to be beyond the fixed stars, and to be the first mover of all the lower spheres.
mobility: Susceptibility of motion; capacity of being moved.
Aptitude to motion; activity; readiness to move.
In cant language, the populace. MOBILIT Y.
moble: To wrap the head in a hood.
moccason: A shoe or cover for the feet, made of deer-skin or other soft leather, without a sole, and ornamented on the upper side; the customary shoe worn by the native Indians.
mocha-stone: Dendritic agate; a mineral in the interior of which appear brown, reddish brown, blackish or green delineations of shrubs destitute of leaves. These in some cases may have been produced by the filtration of the oxyds of iron and manganese; but in other cases they appear to be vegetable fibers, sometimes retaining their natural form and color, and sometimes coated by oxyd of iron.
mock: Properly, to imitate; to mimick; hence, to imitate in contempt or derision; to mimick for the sake of derision; to deride by mimicry.

To deride; to laugh at; to ridicule; to treat with scorn or contempt. As he was going up by the way, there came forth little children out of the city, and mocked him, saying, go up, thou bald head. 2 Kings 2:23; Mark 10:34.
To defeat; to illude; to disappoint; to deceive; as, to mock expectation. Thou hast mocked me and told me lies. Judges 16:10.
To fool; to tantalize; to play on in contempt. He will not . Mock us with his blest sight, then snatch him hence.
To make sport in contempt or in jest, or to speak jestingly. When thou mockest, shall no man make thee ashamed? Job 11:3.
mocked: Imitated or mimicked in derision; laughed at; ridiculed; defeated; illuded.
mocker: One that mocks; a scorner; a scoffer; a derider.
mockery: The act of deriding and exposing to contempt, by mimicking the words or actions of another.

Derision; ridicule; sportive insult or contempt; contemptuous merriment at persons or things. Grace at meals is now generally so performed as to look more like mockery upon devotion, than any solemn application of the mind to God.
Sport; subject of laughter. Of the holy place they made a mockery.
Vain imitation or effort; that which deceives, disappoints or frustrates. It is as the air, invulnerable, And our vain blows malicious mockery.
Imitation; counterfeit appearance; false show. And bear about the mockery of woe . To midnight dances.
mocking: Imitating in contempt; mimicking; ridiculing by mimicry; treating with sneers and scorn; defeating; deluding.
mocking-bird: The mocking thrush of America; a bird of the genus Turdus.
mockingly: By way of derision; in contempt.
mock-lead: A sulphuret of zink, the same as blend, which see.
mockore: A sulphuret of zink, the same as blend, which see.
mock-orange: A plant of the genus Philadelphus.
mock-privet: A plant of the genus Phillyrea.
modal: Consisting in mode only; relating to form; having the form without the essence or reality; as the modal diversity of the faculties of the soul.
modality: The quality of being modal, or being in form only.
mode: Manner of existing or being; manner; method; form; fashion; custom; way; as the mode of speaking; the mode of dressing; modes of receiving or entertaining company. The duty of itself being resolved on, the mode of doing it may be easily found. It is applicable to particular acts, or to a series of acts, or to the common usage of a city of nation. One man has a particular mode of walking; another has a singular mode of dressing his hair. We find it necessary to conform in some measure to the usual modes of dress.
Gradation; degree. What modes of sight between each wide extreme!

In metaphysics, the dependence or affection of a substance. Such complex ideas as contain not in them the supposition of subsisting by themselves, but are considered as dependencies or affections of substances, Locke calls modes. Of these he makes two kinds; simple modes, which are only variations or different combinations of the same idea, as a dozen, which consists of so many units added together; and mixed modes, which are compounded of simple ideas of several kinds, as beauty, which is compounded of color and figure. A mode is that which cannot subsist in and of itself, but is esteemed as belonging to and subsisting by the help of some substance, which for that reason is called its subject.
In music, a regular disposition of the air and accompaniments relative to certain principal sounds, on which a piece of music is formed, and which are called the essential sounds of the mode.
model: A pattern of something to be made; any thing of a particular form, shape or construction, intended for imitation; primarily, a small pattern; a form in miniature of something to be made on a larger scale; as the model of a building; the model of a fort.
A mold; something intended to give shape to castings.
Pattern; example; as, to form a government on the model of the British or American constitution.
modeled: Formed according to a model; planned; shaped; formed.
modeling: Forming according to a model; planning; forming; shaping.
moderate: Literally, limited; restrained; hence, temperate; observing reasonable bounds in indulgence; as moderate in eating or drinking, or in other gratifications.
Limited in quantity; not excessive or expensive. He keeps a moderate table.
Restrained in passion, ardor or temper; not violent; as moderate men of both parties.
Not extreme in opinion; as a moderate Calvinist or Lutheran.
Placed between extremes; holding the mean or middle place; as reformation of a moderate kind.
moderated: Reduced in violence, rigor or intensity; allayed; lessened; tempered; qualified.
moderately: In a middle degree;; not excessively; as water moderately warm. Each nymph but moderately fair.
moderateness: State of being moderate; temperateness; a middle state between extremes; as the moderateness of the weather; used commonly of things, as moderation is of persons.
moderating: Reducing in violence or excess; allaying; tempering; becoming more mild.
moderation: The state of being moderate, or of keeping a due mean between extremes or excess of violence. The General’s moderation after victory was more honorable than the victory itself. In moderation placing all my glory, While tories call me whig, and whigs a tory.
Restraint of violent passions or indulgence of appetite. Eat and drink with moderation; indulge with moderation in pleasures and exercise.
Calmness of mind; equanimity; as, to bear prosperity or adversity with moderation.
moderator: He or that which moderates or restrains. Contemplation is an excellent moderator of the passions.
The person who presides over a meeting or assembly of people to preserve order, propose questions, regulate the proceedings and declare the vote; as the moderator of a town meeting or of a society.
Properly, restrained by a sense of propriety; hence, not forward or bold; not presumptuous or arrogant; not boastful; as a modest youth; a modest man. MODEST.
Not bold or forward; as a modest maid. The word may be thus used without reference to chastity. MODEST. The blushing beauties of a modest maid.W MODEST.
Not loose; not lewd. MODEST. Mrs. Ford, the honest woman, the modest wife. MODEST.
modern: Pertaining to the present time, or time not long past; late; recent; not ancient or remote in past time; as modern days, ages or time; modern authors; modern fashions; modern taste; modern practice.
modernism: Modern practice; something recently formed, particularly in writing.
modernize: To render modern; to adapt ancient compositions to modern persons or things, or rather to adapt the ancient style or idiom to modern style and taste.
modernness: The quality of being modern; recentness; novelty.
moderns: Those who have lived in times recently past, or are now living; opposed to the ancients.
modest: To warn or notify of a fault; to reprove with mildness.
modestly: Not boldly; not arrogantly or presumptuously; with due respect. He modestly expressed his opinions.
Not loosely or wantonly; decently; as, to be modestly attired; to behave modestly.
modesty: That lowly temper which accompanies a moderate estimate of one’s own worth and importance. This temper when natural, springs in some measure from timidity, and in young and inexperienced persons, is allied to bashfulness and diffidence. In persons who have seen the world, and lost their natural timidity, modesty springs no less from principle than from feeling, and is manifested by retiring, unobtrusive manners, assuming less to itself than others are willing to yield, and conceding to others all due honor and respect, or even more than they expect or require.

Modesty, as an act or series of acts, consists in humble, unobtrusive deportment, as opposed to extreme boldness, forwardness, arrogance, presumption, audacity or impudence. Thus we Say, the petitioner urged his claims with modesty; the speaker addressed the audience with modesty.

In females, modesty has the like character as in males; but the word is used also as synonymous with chastity, or purity of manners. In this sense, modesty results from purity of mind, or from the fear of disgrace and ignominy fortified by education and principle. Unaffected modesty is the sweetest charm of female excellence, the richest gem in the diadem of their honor.
modesty-piece: A narrow lace worn by females over the bosom.
modifiable: That may be modified or diversified by various forms and differences; as modifiable matter.
modification: The act of modifying, or giving to any thing new forms, or differences of external qualities or modes. If these powers of cogitation, volition and sensation are not inherent in matter as such, nor acquirable to matter by any motion or modification of it-- .
Particular form or manner; as the various modifications of light or sound. The treaty, in several of its modifications, was held to be objectionable.
Moderated; tempered; qualified in exceptionable parts.
modify: To change the form or external qualities of a thing; to shape; to give a new form of being to; as, to modify matter, light or sound.
To vary; to give a new form to any thing; as, to modify the terms of a contract. A prefix modifies the sense of a verb.
To moderate; to qualify; to reduce in extent or degree. Of his grace . He modifies his first severe decree.
modifying: Changing the external qualities; giving a new form to; moderating.
modillion: modil’yun. In architecture, an ornament in the cornice of the lonic, Corinthian and composite columns; a sort of bracket serving to support the projecture of the larmier or drip; a dental.
modish: According to the mode or customary manner; fashionable; as a modish dress; a modish feast.
modulate: To form sound to a certain key, or to a certain proportion.
To very or inflect sound in a natural, customary or musical manner. Thus the organs of speech modulate the voice in reading or speaking. Could any person so modulate her voice as to deceive so many.
modulated: Formed to a certain key; varied; inflected.
modulating: Forming to a certain proportion; varying; inflecting.
modulation: The act of forming any thing to a certain proportion; as the different proportion and modulation of matter.
The act of inflecting the voice in reading or speaking; a rising or falling of the voice.

In music, the art of composing melody or harmony agreeable to the laws prescribed by any particular key, or of changing the key, or of passing from one key to another. Modulation is the manner of ascertaining and managing the modes; or more generally, the art of conducting the harmony and air through several modes in a manner agreeable to the ear and conformed to rules.
modulator: He or that which modulates. The tongue is a principal modulator of the human voice.
module: To model; to shape; to modulate.
