modus: A compensation for tithes; an equivalent in money or other certain thing, given to a parson or vicar by the owners of land in lieu of tithes. The whole phrase is modus decimandi; but modus alone is commonly used.
mogul: The name of a prince or emperor of the nation in Asia called Moguls or Monguls.
mohair: The hair of a kind of goat in Turkey, of which are made camlets, which are sometimes called by the same name.
mohair-shell: In conchology, a peculiar species of Voluta, of a closely and finely reticulated texture, resembling on the surface mohair, or a close web of the silkworm.
mohammedanisim: The religion or doctrines and precepts of Mohammed, contained in a book called the Koran or Alkoran.
mohammedanize: To render conformable to the modes or principles of the Mohammedans.
mohawk: The appellation given to certain ruffians who infested the streets of London; so called from the nation of Indians of that name in America.
mohock: The appellation given to certain ruffians who infested the streets of London; so called from the nation of Indians of that name in America.
moidore: A gold coin of Portugal, valued at $6, or f1. 7s sterling.
moiety: The half; one of two equal parts; as a moiety of an estate, of goods or of profits; the moiety of a jury or of a nation.
moil: To labor; to toil; to work with painful efforts.
moist: Moderately wet; damp; as a moist atmosphere or air. Exhalation dusk and moist.
Containing water or other liquid in a perceptible degree.
moisten: mois’n. To make damp; to wet in a small degree. A pipe a little moistened on the inside. His bones are moistened with marrow. Job 27:24.
moistened: mois’nd. Made wet in a small degree.
moistener: mois’ner. He or that which moistens.
moistness: Dampness; a small degree of wetness.
moisture: A moderate degree of wetness. Set such plants as require much moisture, on sandy, dry grounds.
Asmall quantity of any liquid; as the moisture of the body.
molar: Having power to grind; grinding; as the molar teeth.
mold: Fine soft earth, or earth easily pulverized, such as constitutes soil; as black mold. A mortal substance of terrestrial mold.
A substance like down which forms on bodies which lie long in warm and damp air. The microscope exhibits this substance as consisting of small plants.
Matter of which any thing is formed. Nature formed me of her softest mold.

The matrix in which any thing is cast and receives its form. Molds are of various kinds. Molds for casting cannon and various vessels, are composed of some species of earth, particularly clay. Molds for other purposes consist of a cavity in some species of metal, cut or formed to the shape designed, or are otherwise formed, each for its particular use.
Cast; form; as a writer of vulgar mold.
moldable: That may be molded or formed.
molded: Formed into a particular shape; kneaded.
molder: He who molds or forms into shape.

To turn to dust by natural decay; to crumble; to perish; to waste away by a gradual separation of the component particles, without the presence of water. In this manner, animal and vegetable substances molder, and so also do stones and shells. When statues molder, and when arches fall.
To be diminished; to waste away gradually. If he had sat still, the enemy’s army would have moldered to nothing.
To turn to dust; to crumble; to waste. Some felt the silent stroke of moldering age.
moldering: Turning to dust; crumbling; wasting away.
molding: Any thing cast in a mold, or which appears to be so; hence, in architecture, a projecture beyond the wall, column, wainscotan assemblage of which forms a cornice, a door-case, or other decoration.
mold-warp: A mole; a small animal of the genus Talpa, that moves under ground and turns up the mold or surface of the earth.
mole: A spot, mark or small permanent protuberance on the human body, from which usually issue one or more hairs.
A mass of fleshy matter of a spherical figure, generated in the uterus.

A mound or massive work formed of large stones laid in the sea by means of coffer dams, extended either in a right line or an arch of a circle before a port, which it serves to defend from the violent impulse of the waves; thus protecting ships in a harbor. The word is sometimes used for the harbor itself.
Among the Romans, a kind of mausoleum, built like a round tower on a square base, insulated, encompassed with columns and covered with a dome.

A small animal of the genus Talpa, which in search of worms or other insects, forms a road just under the surface of the ground, raising the soil into a little ridge; from which circumstance it is called a mold-warp, or mold-turner. The mole has very small eyes. Learn of the mole to plow, the worm to weave.
mole-cast: A little elevation of earth made by a mole.
mole-catcher: One whose employment is to catch moles.
mole-cricket: An insect of the genus Gryllus.
molecule: A very minute particle of matter. Molecules are elementary, constituent, or integrant. The latter result from the union of the elementary.
mole-hill: A little hillock or elevation of earth thrown up by moles working under ground; hence proverbially, a very small hill, or other small thing, compared with a larger. --Having leaped over such mountains, lie down before a mole- hill .
molest: To trouble; to disturb; to render uneasy. They have molested the church with needless opposition.
mole-track: The course of a mole under ground.
molinist: A follower of the opinions of Molina, a Spanish Jesuit, in respect to grace; an opposer of the Jansenists.
mollification: The act of mollifying or softening.
mollifier: That which softens, appeases or mitigates.
He that softens, mitigates or pacifies.
mollify: To soften; to make soft or tender. Isaiah 1:6.
To assuage, as pain or irritation.
To appease; to pacify; to calm or quiet.
To qualify; to reduce in harshness or asperity.
mollusca: In zoology, a division or class of animals whose bodies are soft, without an internal skeleton, or articulated covering. Some of them breathe by lungs, others by gills; some live on land, others in water. Some of them are naked; others testaceous or provided with shells. Many of them are furnished with feelers or tentacula.
molluscan: Pertaining to the mollusca, or partaking of their properties.
molluscous: Pertaining to the mollusca, or partaking of their properties.
molossus: In Greek and Latin verse, a foot of three long syllables.
molt: To shed or cast the hair, feathers, skin, horns; as an animal. Fowls molt by losing their feathers, beasts by losing their hair, serpents by casting their skins, and deer their horns. The molting of the hawk is called mewing.
molten: a. Made of melted metal; as a molten image.
molting: Casting or shedding a natural covering, as hair, feathers, skin or horns.
The act or operation by which certain animals, annually or at certain times, cast off or lose their hair, feathers, skins, horns
moly: Wild garlic, a plant having a bulbous root.
molybden: An ore of molybdenum, a scarce mineral of a peculiar form, and sometimes confounded with plumbago, from which however it is distinguished by its more shining, scaly appearance, and a more greasy feel.
molybdena: An ore of molybdenum, a scarce mineral of a peculiar form, and sometimes confounded with plumbago, from which however it is distinguished by its more shining, scaly appearance, and a more greasy feel.
molybdenous: Pertaining to molybden, or obtained from it. The molybdenous acid is the deutoxyd of molybdenum.
molybdenum: A metal which has not been reduced into masses of any magnitude, but has been obtained only in small separate globules, in a blackish, brilliant mass. These are brittle and extremely infusible. The most common natural compound of this metal is a sulphuret.
mome: A dull, silent person; a stupid fellow; a stock; a post.
moment: The most minute and indivisible part of time; an instant. In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye. 7 Corinthians 15:52.
Force; impulsive power. --Touch with lightest moment of impulse, His free will. Little used; but hence,
Importance in influence or effect; consequence; weight or value. It is an abstruse speculation, but also of far less moment to us than the others.
momentary: Done in a moment; continuing only a moment; lasting a very short time; as a momentary pang. Momentary as a sound, Swift as a shadow, short as any dream.
momently: In a moment; every moment. We momently expect the arrival of the mail.
momentous: Important; weighty; of consequence. Let no false step be made in the momentous concerns of the soul.
momentum: In mechanics, impetus; the quantity of motion in a moving body. This is always equal to the quantity of matter multiplied into the velocity.
mommery: An entertainment or frolick in masks; a farcical entertainment in which masked persons play antic tricks.
mummery: An entertainment or frolick in masks; a farcical entertainment in which masked persons play antic tricks.
Masking; sport; diversion; frolicking in masks; low contemptible amusement; buffoonery. Your fathers . Disdained the mummery of foreign strollers.
Farcical show; hypocritical disguise and parade to delude vulgar minds.
momot: The name of a genus of birds in S. America, whose beak and tongue resemble the toucan’s.
monachal: Pertaining to monks or a monastic life; monastic.
monachism: The state of monks; a monastic life.
monad: An ultimate atom, or simple unextended point.
monadelph: In botany, a plant whose stamens are united in one body by the filaments.
monadelphian: Having the stamens united in one body by the filaments.
monadic: Having the nature or character of a monad.
monadical: Having the nature or character of a monad.
monander: In botany, a plant having one stamen only.
monarch: The prince or ruler of a nation, who exercises all the powers of government without control, or who is vested with absolute sovereign power; an emperor, king or prince invested with an unlimited power. This is the strict sense of the word.

A king or prince, the supreme magistrate of a nation, whose powers are in some respects limited by the constitution of the government. Thus we call the king of Great Britain a monarch, although he can make no law without the consent of parliament.
He or that which is superior to others of the same kind; as, an oak is called the monarch of the forest; a lion the monarch of wild beasts.
One that presides; president; as Bacchus, monarch of the vine.
Supreme; ruling; as a monarch savage.
monarchal: Pertaining to a monarch; suiting a monarch; sovereign; regal; imperial. Satan, whom now transcendent glory raised . Above his fellows, with monarchial pride-- .
monarchic: Vested in a single ruler; as monarchical government or power.
monarchical: Vested in a single ruler; as monarchical government or power.
monarchize: To play the king; to act the monarch.
monarchy: A state or government in which the supreme power is lodged in the hands of a single person. Such a state is usually called an empire or a kingdom; and we usually give this denomination to a large state only. But the same name is sometimes given to a kingdom or state in which the power of the king or supreme magistrate is limited by a constitution, or by fundamental laws. Such is the British monarchy. Hence we speak of absolute or despotic monarchies, and of limited monarchies. A free government has a great advantage over a simple monarchy.
monastery: A house of religious retirement, or of seclusion from ordinary temporal concerns, whether an abbey, a priory or a nunnery. The word is usually applied to the houses of monks, mendicant friars and nuns.
monastic: Pertaining to monasteries, monks and nuns; recluse; secluded from the temporal concerns of life and devoted to religion; as a monastic life; monastic orders.
monastical: Pertaining to monasteries, monks and nuns; recluse; secluded from the temporal concerns of life and devoted to religion; as a monastic life; monastic orders.
monastically: Reclusely; in a retired manner; in the manner of monks.
monday: The second day of the week.
monde: The world; also, a glove, an ensign of authority.
monecian: In botany, one of that class of plants, whose male and female flowers are on the same plant.
Pertaining to the class of plants above described.
money: Coin; stamped metal; any piece of metal, usually gold, silver or copper, stamped by public authority, and used as the medium of commerce. We sometimes give the name of money to other coined metals, and to any other material which rude nations use a medium of trade. But among modern commercial nations, gold, silver and copper are the only metals used for this purpose. Gold and silver, containing great value in small compass, and being therefore of easy conveyance, and being also durable and little liable to diminution by use, are the most convenient metals for coin or money, which is the representative of commodities of all kinds, of lands, and of every thing that is capable of being transferred in commerce.

Bank notes or bills of credit issued by authority, and exchangeable for coin or redeemable, are also called money; as such notes in modern times represent coin, and are used as a substitute for it. If a man pays in hand for goods in bank notes which are current, he is said to pay in ready money.
Wealth; affluence. Money can neither open new avenues to pleasure, nor block up the passages of anguish.
moneyage: Anciently, in England, a general land tax levied by the two first Norman kings, a shilling on each hearth.
money-bag: A bag or purse for holding money. MONEY-BOxA box or till to hold money.
money-broker: A broker who deals in money.
money-changer: A broker who deals in money or exchanges.
moneyed: Rich in money; having money; able to command money; used often in opposition to such as have their wealth in real estate. Invite moneyed men to lend to the merchants.
Consisting in money; as moneyed capital.
moneyer: A banker; one who deals in money.
money-matter: An account consisting of charges of money; an account between debtor and creditor.
money-scrivener: A person who raises money for others.
money-wort: A plant of the genus Lysimachia.
monger: A trader; a dealer; now used only or chiefly in composition; as a fish- monger, iron-monger, news-monger, cheese-monger.
mongrel: Of a mixed breed; of different kinds.
An animal of a mixed breed.
moniment: An inscription; something to preserve memory.
A mark; an image; a superscription.
monition: Warning; instruction given by way of caution; as the monitions of a friend.
Information; indication. We have no visible monitions of other periods, such as we have of the day by successive light and darkness.
monitor: One who warns of faults or informs of duty; one who gives advice and instruction by way of reproof or caution. You need not be a monitor to the king.
In schools, a person authorized to look to the scholars in the absence of the instructor, or to notice the absence of faults of the scholars; or to instruct a division of class.
monitorial: Performed by monitors or a monitor; as monitorial instruction.
Conducted by or under the instruction of monitors, or subordinate teachers; as monitorial schools. WONITORIAL.
monitory: Giving admonition; warning; instructing by way of caution. Losses, miscarriages and disappointments are monitory and instructive.
monk: A man who retires from the ordinary temporal concerns of the world, and devotes himself to religion. Monks usually live in monasteries, on entering which they take a vow to observe certain rules. Some however live as hermits in solitude, and others have lived a strolling life without any fixed residence.
monkery: The life of monks; the monastic life.
monkey: The popular name of the ape and baboon. But in zoology, monkey is more properly the name of those animals of the genus Simia, which have long tails. Ray distributes animals of this kind into three classes; apes which have no tails; monkeys with long tails; and baboons with short tails.
Aname of contempt or of slight kindness.
monkish: Like a monk, or pertaining to monks; monastic; as monkish manners; monkish dress; monkish solitude.
monochord: A musical instrument of one string. As its name imports, it had originally but one string; but it is generally constructed with two, by means of which the musician is better enabled to try the proportions of sounds and intervals, and judge of the harmony of two tempered notes. In the proper sense of the word, a trumpet marine is considered a monochord.
monochromatic: Consisting of one color, or presenting rays of light of one color only.
monocotyle: Having only one seed-lobe or seminal leaf.
monocotyledonous: Having only one seed-lobe or seminal leaf.
monocotyledon: In botany, a plant with only one cotyledon or seed-lobe.
monodactylous: Having one toe only, as an animal.
monodon: The unicorn fish, or sea-unicorn, which has a remarkable horn projecting from its head. It is called also the monoceros, or horned narwhal. Its usual size is form sixteen to twenty feet.
monody: A song or poem sung by one person only.
monogam: In botany, a plant that has a simple flower, though the anthers are united.
monogamian: Pertaining to the order of plants that have a simple flower.
monogamous: Having one wife only and not permitted to marry a second.
monogamy: The marriage of one wife only, or the state of such as are restrained to a single wife.
monogram: A character or cypher composed of one, two or more letters interwoven, being an abbreviation of a name; used on seals
monogrammal: Sketching in the manner of a monogram.
monograph: An account or description of a single thing or class of things; as a monograph of violets in botany; a monograph of an Egyptian mummy.
monography: A description drawn in lines without colors.
monogyn: In botany, a plant having only one style or stigma.
monogynian: Pertaining to the order monogynia; having only one style or stigma.
monologue: A soliloquy; a speech uttered by a person alone.
A poem, song or scene composed for a single performer.
monome: In algebra, a quantity that has one name only.
monomial: In algebra, a quantity expressed by one name or letter.
monopetalous: In botany, having only one petal, or a one-petaled corol; as a monopetalous corol or flower.
monophysite: One who maintains that Jesus Christ had but one nature, or that the human and divine nature were so united as to form one nature only.
monopolist: One that monopolizes; a person who engrosses a commodity by purchasing the whole of that article in market for the purpose of selling it at an advanced price; or one who has a license or privilege granted by authority, for the sole buying or selling of any commodity. The man who retains in his hands his own produce or manufacture, is not a monopolist within the meaning of the laws for preventing monopolies.
monopolizer: One that monopolizes; a person who engrosses a commodity by purchasing the whole of that article in market for the purpose of selling it at an advanced price; or one who has a license or privilege granted by authority, for the sole buying or selling of any commodity. The man who retains in his hands his own produce or manufacture, is not a monopolist within the meaning of the laws for preventing monopolies.
monopolize: To purchase or obtain possession of the whole of any commodity or goods in market with the view of selling them at advanced prices, and of having the power of commanding the prices; as, to monopolize sugar or tea.

To engross or obtain by any means the exclusive right of trading to any place, and the sole power of vending any commodity or goods in a particular place or country; as, to monopolize the India or Levant trade.
To obtain the whole; as, to monopolize advantages.
monopoly: The sole power of vending any species of goods, obtained either by engrossing the articles in market by purchase, or by a license from the government confirming this privilege. Thus the East India Company in Great Britain has a monopoly of the trade to the East Indies, granted to them by charter. Monopolies by individuals obtained by engrossing, are an offense prohibited by law. But a man has by natural right the exclusive power of vending his own produce or manufactures, and to retain that exclusive right is not a monopoly within the meaning of law.
monoptote: A noun having only one oblique case.
monostich: A composition consisting of one verse only.
monostrophic: Having one strophe only; not varied in measure; written in unvaried measure.
monosyllabic: Consisting of one syllable; as a monosyllabic word.
Consisting of words of one syllable; as a monosyllabic verse.
monotheism: The doctrine or belief of the existence of one God only.
monothelite: One who holds that Christ had but one will.
monotone: In rhetoric, a sameness of sound, or the utterance of successive syllables on one unvaried key, without inflection or cadence.
monotonous: Continued in the same tone without inflection or cadence; unvaried in tone.
monotonously: With one uniform tone; without inflection of voice.
monotony: Uniformity of tone or sound; want of inflections of voice in speaking; want of cadence or modulation.
Uniformity; sameness. At sea, every thing that breaks the monotony of the surrounding expanse attracts attention.
monsoon: A periodical wind, blowing six months from the same quarter or point of the compass, then changing and blowing the same time from the opposite quarter. The monsoons prevail in the East Indies, and are called also trade winds. But we usually give the denomination of trade winds to those which blow the whole year from the same point, as the winds within the tropics on the Atlantic.
monster: An animal produced with a shape or with parts that are not natural, as when the body is ill formed or distorted, or the limbs too few or too many, or when any part is extravagantly out of proportion, either through defect or excess.
Any unnatural production; something greatly deformed. Monsters are common in the vegetable kingdom.
A person so wicked as to appear horrible; one unnaturally wicked or mischievous. So a parricide is called a monster.
monstrosity: The state of being monstrous, or out of the common order of nature. We often read of monstrous births; but we see a greater monstrosity in education, when a father begets a son and trains him up into a beast.

An unnatural production; that which is monstrous. Fabri arranges distortions, gibbosities, tumorsin the class of morbific monstrosities. A monstrosity never changes the name or affects the immutability of a species.
monstrous: Unnatural in form; out of the common course of nature; as a monstrous birth or production.
Strange; very wonderful; generally expressive of dislike.
Enormous; huge; extraordinary; as a monstrous highth; a monstrous tree or mountain.
Shocking to the sight or other senses; hateful.
Exceedingly; very much; as monstrous hard; monstrous thick. And will be monstrous witty on the poor.
monstrously: In a manner out of the common order of nature; hence, shockingly; terribly; hideously; horribly; as a man monstrously wicked.
To a great degree; enormously; extravagantly. Who with his wife is monstrously in love.
montanic: Pertaining to mountains; consisting in mountains.
montanist: A follower of the heresiarch Montanus, a Phrygian by birth, who pretended he was inspired by the Holy Spirit and instructed in several points not revealed to the apostles. His sect sprung up in the second century.
montanistic: Pertaining to the heresy of Montanus.
montanize: To follow the opinions of Montanus.
monteth: A vessel in which glasses are washed; so called from the name of the inventor.
month: A space or period of time constituting a division of the year. Month originally signified the time of one revolution of the moon, a lunation, or the period from one change or conjunction of the moon with the sun to another, a period of 27 days, 7 hours, 43 minutes and 5 seconds. This is the periodical month, or as we generally call it, the lunar month. In this sense we still use the word month. But we also apply the term to the space of time in which the sun passes through one sign, or a twelfth part of the zodiac. This period contains 30 days, 10 hours, 29 minutes, 5 seconds, and it called a solar month. In the year, there are twelve solar months, and thirteen lunar months.

A space or period of time constituting a division of the year. Month originally signified the time of one revolution of the moon, a lunation, or the period from one change or conjunction of the moon with the sun to another, a period of 27 days, 7 hours, minutes and 5 seconds. This is the periodical month, or as we generally call it, the lunar month. In this sense we still use the word month. But we also apply the term to the space of time in which the sun passes through one sign, or a twelfth part of the zodiac. This period contains 30 days, 10 hours, 29 minutes, seconds, and it called a solar month. In the year, there are twelve solar months, and thirteen lunar months. In popular language, four weeks are called a month, being nearly the length of the lunar month. A calendar month differs in some degree from a solar month; consisting of twenty eight, twenty nine, thirty or thirty one day
monthly: Continued a month or performed in a month; as the monthly revolution of the moon.
Done or happening once a month, or every month; as the monthly concert of prayer; a monthly visit.
Once a month; in every month. The moon changes monthly.
As if under the influence of the moon; in the manner of a lunatic.
montmartrite: A mineral of a yellowish color, occurring massive, and found at Montmartre, near Paris. It is soft, but resists the weather. It is a compound of the sulphate and carbonate of lime.
montoir: In horsemanship, a stone used for aiding to mount a horse.
monument: Any thing by which the memory of a person or an event is preserved or perpetuated; a building, stone or other thing placed or erected to remind men of the person who raised it, or of a person deceased, or of any remarkable event; as a mausoleum, a pillar, a pyramid, a triumphal arch, a tombstone and the like. A pillar of feet in highth, composed of Portland stone, was erected in London as a monument to preserve the memory of the great conflagration in 1666. A monument is erected on Bunker Hill to commemorate the battle of June 17, 1775.
A stone or a heap of stones or other durable thing, intended to mark the bounds of states, towns or distinct possessions, and preserve the memory of divisional lines.
A thing that reminds or gives notice.
monumental: Pertaining to a monument; as a monumental inscription.
Serving as a monument; memorial; preserving memory. Of pine or monumental oak. A work outlasting monumental brass.
Belonging to a tomb; as a monumental rest.W .
mood: The form of an argument; the regular determination of propositions according to their quantity, as universal or particular, and their quality, as affirmative or negative.

The variation of a verb to express manner of action or being. [See Mode.; . In the foregoing senses, and in all cases, this word when derived from the Latin modus, ought to be written mode, it being a distinct word from the following.
Temper of mind; temporary state of the mind in regard to passion or feeling; humor; as a melancholy mood; an angry mood; a suppliant mood.
moody: Angry; peevish; fretful; out of humor. Every peevish moody malcontent.
moon: The heavenly orb which revolves round the earth; a secondary planet or satellite of the earth, whose borrowed light is reflected to the earth and serves to dispel the darkness of night. Its mean distance from the earth is 60 1/2 semidiameters of the earth, or 240,000 miles. Its revolution round the earth in 27 days, 7 hours, minutes, constitutes the lunar month.
A month. This is the sense in which rude nations use the name of the moon; as seven moons. Half-moon, in fortification, a figure resembling a crescent.
moon-beam: A ray of light from the moon.
moon-calf: A mole or mass of fleshy matter generated in the uterus.
moon-eye: An eye affected by the moon.
moon-eyed: Having eyes affected by the revolutions of the moon.
moon-fish: A fish whose tail is shaped like a half-moon.
moonlight: The light afforded by the moon.
Illuminated by the moon; as moonlight revels.
moon-sad: A plant of the genus Menispermum, having a rosaceous flower.
moonshine: In burlesque, a month. A matter of moonshine, a matter of no consequence or of indifference.
Illuminated by the moon; as a fair moonshine night. I went to see them in a moonshiny night.
moonshiny: Illuminated by the moon; as a fair moonshine night. I went to see them in a moonshiny night.
moonstone: A variety of adularia, of a white color, or a yellowish or greenish white, somewhat iridescent, found in blunt amorphous masses, or crystallized in truncated rhomboidal prisms, or in rectangular tables, or in hexahedral prisms beveled at both ends. The surface is often sulcated.
moonstruck: Affected by the influence of the moon; lunatic; as moonstruck madness.
moon-trefoil: A plant of the genus Medicago.
moon-wort: A plant of the genus Lunaria; satin-flower; honesty.
moony: Lunated; having a crescent for a standard; in resemblance of the moon; as the moony troops or moony host of the sultans of Turkey.
moor: A tract of land overrun with heath.
A marsh; a fen; a tract of wet low ground, or ground covered with stagnant water.

A native of the northern coast of Africa, called by the Romans from the color of the people, Mauritania, the country of dark-complexioned people. The same country is now called Morocco, Tunis, Algiers
To confine or secure a ship in a particular station, as by cables and anchors or by chains. A ship is never said to be moored, when she rides by a single anchor.
To be confined by cables or chains. On oozy ground his galleys moor.
moorcock: A fowl of the genus Tetrao, found in moors; red-game; gor-cock.
moorfowl: A fowl of the genus Tetrao, found in moors; red-game; gor-cock.
moorhen: A fowl of the genus Tetrao, found in moors; red-game; gor-cock.
moored: Made fast in a station by cables or chains.
mooring: Confining to a station by cables or chains.
In seamen’s language, moorings are the anchors, chains and bridles laid athwart the bottom of a river or harbor to confine a ship.
moorish: Marshy; fenny; watery. Along the moorish fens.
Pertaining to the Moors in Africa.
moorland: A marsh or tract of low water ground.
Land rising into moderate hills, foul, cold and full of bogs, as in Staffordshire, England.
moory: Marshy; fenny; boggy; watery. As when thick mists arise from moory vales.
moose: An animal of the genus Cervus, and the largest of the deer kind, growing sometimes to the highth of 17 hands, and weighing pounds. This animal has palmated horns, with a short thick neck, and an upright mane of a light brown color. The eyes are small, the ears a foot long, very broad and slouching; the upper lip is square, hangs over the lower one, and has a deep sulcus in the middle so as to appear bifid. This animal inhabits cold northern climates, being found in the American forests of Canada and New England, and in the corresponding latitudes of Europe and Asia. It is the elk of Europe.
moot: To debate; to discuss; to argue for and against. The word is applied chiefly to the disputes of students in law, who state a question and discuss it by way of exercise to qualify themselves for arguing causes in court.
To argue or plead on a supposed cause.
A point, case or question to be mooted or debated; a disputable case; an unsettled question. In this moot-case your judgment to refuse.
moot-case: A point, case or question to be mooted or debated; a disputable case; an unsettled question. In this moot-case your judgment to refuse.
moot-point: A point, case or question to be mooted or debated; a disputable case; an unsettled question. In this moot-case your judgment to refuse.
mooter: A disputer of a mooted case.
moot-hall: A town hall; hall of judgment.
moot-house: A town hall; hall of judgment.
mop: A piece of cloth, or a collection of thrums or coarse yarn fastened to a handle and used for cleaning floors.
To rub or wipe with a mop.
mope: To be very stupid; to be very dull; to drowse; to be spiritless or gloomy. Demoniac phrensy, moping melancholy. --Or but a sickly part of one true sense . Could not so mope.
A stupid or low spirited person; a drone.
moped: Made stupid. A young, low spirited, moped creature.
moppet: A rag-baby; a puppet made of cloth; a fondling name of a little girl.
mopsey: A rag-baby; a puppet made of cloth; a fondling name of a little girl.
moral: Relating to the practice, manners or conduct of men as social beings in relation to each other, and with reference to right and wrong. The word moral is applicable to actions that are good or evil, virtuous or vicious, and has reference to the law of God as the standard by which their character is to be determined. The word however may be applied to actions which affect only, or primarily and principally, a person’s own happiness. Keep at the least within the compass of moral actions, which have in them vice or virtue. Mankind is broken loose from moral bands.
Subject to the moral law and capable of moral actions; bound to perform social duties; as a moral agent or being.

Supported by the evidence of reason or probability; founded on experience of the ordinary course of things; as moral certainty, distinguished from physical or mathematical certainty or demonstration. Physical and mathematical certainty may be stiled infallible, and moral certainty may be properly stiled indubitable. WORAL. Things of a moral nature may be proved by moral arguments.
Conformed to rules of right, or to the divine law respecting social duties; virtuous; just; as when we say, a particular action is not moral.
Conformed to law and right in exterior deportment; as, he leads a good moral life.
moralist: One who teaches the duties of life, or a writer of essays intended to correct vice and inculcate moral duties.
One who practices moral duties; a mere moral person.
morality: The doctrine or system of moral duties, or the duties of men in their social character; ethics. The system of morality to be gathered from the writings of ancient sages, falls very short of that delivered in the gospel.
The practice of the moral duties; virtue. We often admire the politeness of men whose morality we question.

The quality of an action which renders it good; the conformity of an act to the divine law, or to the principles of rectitude. This conformity implies that the act must be performed by a free agent, and from a motive of obedience to the divine will. This is the strict theological and scriptural sense of morality. But we often apply the word to actions which accord with justice and human laws, without reference to the motives form which they proceed.
moralization: Moral reflections, or the act of making moral reflections.
moralize: To apply to a moral purpose, or to explain in a moral sense. This fable is moralized in a common proverb. Did he not moralize this spectacle?
To furnish with manners or examples.
To render moral or virtuous; to correct the morals of. It had a large share in moralizing the poor white people of the country.
To speak or write on moral subjects, or to make moral reflections.
moralized: Applied to a moral purpose, or explained in a moral sense.
moralizing: Applying to a moral purpose, or explaining in a moral sense.
Making moral reflections in words or writing.
The application of facts to a moral purpose, or the making of moral reflections. His moralizings are always pleasant, and he does not spare, where he thinks it useful to moralize.
morally: In a moral or ethical sense; according to the rules of morality. By good, morally so called, bonum honestum ought chiefly to be understood.
Virtuously; honestly; according to moral rules in external department. He resolves to live morally.

According to the rules of the divine law. An action is not in strictness morally good, which does not proceed from good motives, or a principle of love and obedience to the divine law and to the lawgiver. Charity bestowed to gratify pride, or justice done by compulsion, cannot be morally good in the sight of God.

According to the evidence of human reason or of probabilities, founded on facts or experience; according to the usual course of things and human judgment. It is morally impossible for a hypocrite to keep himself long on his guard. From the nature of things, I am morally certain that a mind free from passion and prejudice is more fit to pass a true judgment than one biased by affection and interest.
morals: The practice of the duties of life; as a man of correct morals.

Conduct; behavior; course of life, in regard to good and evil. Some, as corrupt in their morals as vice could make them, have been solicitous to have their children virtuously and piously educated. What can laws do without morals?
morass: A marsh; a fen; a tract of low moist ground.
moravian: One of a religious sect, called the United Brethren.
morbid: Diseased; sickly; not sound and healthful; as morbid humors; a morbid constitution; a morbid state of the juices of a plant; a morbid sensibility.
morbidness: A state of being diseased, sickly or unsound.
morbific: Causing disease; generating a sickly state; as morbific matter.
morbifical: Causing disease; generating a sickly state; as morbific matter.
morbillous: Pertaining to the measles; measly; partaking of the nature of measles, or resembling the eruptions of that disease.
morbose: Proceeding from disease; unsound; unhealthy; as a morbose tumor or excrescence in plants.
mordant: A substance which has a chimical affinity for coloring matter and serves to fix colors; such as alum.
mordicant: Biting; acrid; as the mordicant quality of a body.
mordication: The act of biting or corroding; corrosion. Another cause is the mordication of the orifices, especially of the mesentery veins.
more: Greater in quality, degree or amount; in a general sense; as more land; more water; more courage; more virtue; more power or wisdom; more love; more praise; more light. It is applicable to every thing, material or immaterial.
Greater in number; exceeding in numbers; as more men; more virtues; more years. The children of Israel are more than we.Exodus 1:9.
Greater. The more part knew not why they had come together. Acts 19:32.
Added to some former number; additional. But Montague demands one labor more.
To a greater degree. Israel loved Joseph more than all his children. Genesis 37:3.
morel: Garden nightshade, a plant of the genus Solanum.
moreover: Beyond what has been said; further; besides; also; likewise. Moreover, by them is thy servant warned. Psalm 19:11.
moresk: Done after the manner of the Moors.
A species of painting or carving done after the Moorish manner, consisting of grotesque pieces and compartments promiscuously interspersed.
moresque: Done after the manner of the Moors.
morgray: A Mediterranean fish of a pale reddish gray color, spotted with brown and white. It is called also the rough hound-fish. It weighs about twenty ounces and is well tasted.
moril: A mushroom of the size of a walnut, abounding with little holes.
morilliform: Having the form of the moril, a mushroom.
morillon: A fowl of the genus Anas.
morion: Armor for the head; a helmet or casque to defend the head.
morisco: A dance, or a dancer of the morris or moorish dance.
morisk: A dance, or a dancer of the morris or moorish dance.
morkin: Among hunters, a beast that has died by sickness or mischance.
morling: Wool plucked from a dead sheep.
mortling: Wool plucked from a dead sheep.
morn: The first part of the day; the morning; a word used chiefly in poetry. And blooming peach shall ever bless thy morn.
morning: The first part of the day, beginning at twelve o’clock at night and extending to twelve at noon. Thus we say, a star rises at one o'clock in the morning. In a more limited sense, morning is the time beginning an hour or two before sunrise, or at break of day, and extending to the hour of breakfast and of beginning the labors of the day. Among men of business in large cities, the morning extends to the hour of dining.
The first or early part. In the morning of life, devote yourself to the service of the Most High.

Pertaining to the first part or early part of the day; being in the early part of the day; as morning dew; morning light; morning service. She looks as clear' . As morning roses newly washed with dew.
morning-gown: A gown worn in the morning before one is formally dressed.
morning-star: The planet Venus, when it precedes the sun in rising, and shines in the morning.
morocco: A fine kind of leather; leather dressed in a particular manner; said to be borrowed from the Moors.
morose: Of a sour temper; severe; sullen and austere. M. Some have deserved censure for a morose and affected taciturnity; others have made speeches though they had nothing to say.
moroseness: Sourness of temper; sullenness. Moroseness is not precisely peevishness or fretfulness, though often accompanied with it. It denotes more of silence and severity or ill humor, than the irritability or irritation which characterizes peevishness. Learn good humor, never to oppose without just reason; abate some degrees of pride and moroseness.
moroxylic: Moroxylic acid is obtained from a saline exsudation from the morrus alba or white mulberry.
morphia: A vegetable alkali extracted from opium, of which it constitutes the narcotic principle.
morrice: A moorish dance; a dance in imitation of the Moors, as sarabands, chaconsusually performed with castanets, tamboursby young men in their shirts, with bells at their feet and ribbons of various colors tied round their arms and flung across their shoulders. Nine men’s morrice, a kind or play with nine holes in the ground.
morris: A moorish dance; a dance in imitation of the Moors, as sarabands, chaconsusually performed with castanets, tamboursby young men in their shirts, with bells at their feet and ribbons of various colors tied round their arms and flung across their shoulders. Nine men’s morrice, a kind or play with nine holes in the ground.
morris-dance: A moorish dance; a dance in imitation of the Moors, as sarabands, chaconsusually performed with castanets, tamboursby young men in their shirts, with bells at their feet and ribbons of various colors tied round their arms and flung across their shoulders. Nine men’s morrice, a kind or play with nine holes in the ground.
morrow: The day next after the present. Till this stormy night is gone, And th’ eternal morrow dawn. This word is often preceded by on or to. The Lord did that thing on the morrow. Exodus 9:6. To morrow shall this sign be. Exodus 8:23. So we Say, to night, to day. To morrow is equivalent to on the morrow.

The next day subsequent to any day specified. But if the sacrifice of his offering shall be a vow or a voluntary offering, it shall be eaten the same day that he offereth his sacrifice; and on the morrow also the remainder of it shall be eaten. Leviticus 7:16. Good morrow, a term of salutation; good morning.
morse: In zoology, the sea-horse, or walrus, an animal of the genus Trichechus, which sometimes grows to the length of 18 feet. This animal has a round head, small mouth and eyes, thick lips, a short neck, and a body thick in the middle and tapering towards the tail. His skin is wrinkled, with short hairs thinly dispersed. His legs are short and loosely articulated, and he has five toes on each foot connected by webs. Teeth of this animal have been found which weighed thirty pounds. These animals are gregarious, but shy and very fierce when attacked. They inhabit the shores of Spitzbergen, Hudson’s bay and other places in high northern latitudes.
morsel: A bite; a mouthful; a small piece of food. Every morsel to a satisfied hunger is only a new labor to a tired digestion.
A piece; a meal; something to be eaten. On these herbs and fruits and flowers . Feed first, on each beast next and fish and fowl, No homely morsels.
A small quantity of something not eatable.
mort: A tune sounded at the death of game.
mortal: Subject to death; destined to die. Man is mortal.

Deadly; destructive to life; causing death, or that must cause death; as a mortal wound; mortal poison. The fruit . Of that forbidden tree whose mortal tastel . Brought death into the world, and all our woe-- .
Bringing death; terminating life. Safe in the hand of one disposing power, Or in the natal or the mortal hour.
Deadly in malice or purpose; as a mortal foe. In colloquial language, a mortal foe is an inveterate foe.
Exposing to certain death; incurring the penalty of death; condemned to be punished with death; not venial; as a mortal sin,
mortality: Subjection to death or the necessity of dying. When I saw her die, I then did think on your mortality.
Death. Gladly would I meet . Mortality, my sentence.
Frequency of death; actual death of great numbers of men or beasts; as a time of great mortality: .
Human nature. Take these tears, mortality’s relief.
Power of destruction. Mortality and mercy in Vienna, Live in thy tongue and heart.
mortally: Irrecoverably; in a manner that must cause death; as mortally wounded.
Extremely. Adrian mortally envied poets, painters and artificers, in works wherein he had a vein to excel.
mortar: A vessel of wood or metal in form of an inverted bell, in which substances are pounded or bruised with a pestle.
A short piece of ordnance, thick and wide, used for throwing bombs, carcasses, shells; so named from its resemblance in shape to the utensil above described.

A mixture of lime and sand with water, used as a cement for uniting stones and bricks in walls. If the lime is slaked and the materials mixed with lime water, the cement will be much stronger. Mort d’ancestor. In law, a writ of assize, by which a demandant recovers possession of an estate from which he has been ousted, on the death of his ancestor.
mortgage: mor’gage. To grant an estate in fee as security for money lent or contracted to be paid ata certain time, on condition that if the debt shall be discharged according to the contract, the grant shall be void, otherwise to remain in full force. It is customary to give a mortgage for securing the repayment of money lent, or the payment of the purchase money of an estate, or for any other debt.

Literally, a dead pledge; the grant of an estate in fee as security for the payment of money, and on the condition that if the money shall be paid according to the contract, the grant shall be void, and the mortgagee shall re-convey the estate to the mortgager. Formerly the condition was, that if the mortgager should repay the money at the day specified, he might then re-enter on the estate granted in pledge; but the modern practice is for the mortgagee, on receiving payment, to reconvey the land to the mortgager. Before the time specified for payment, that is, between the time of contract and the time limited for payment, the estate is conditional, and the mortgagee is called tenant in mortgage; but on failure of payment at the time limited, the estate becomes absolute in the mortgagee. But in this case, courts of equity interpose, and if the estate is of more value than the debt, they
The state of being pledged; as lands given in mortgage.
A pledge of goods or chattels by a debtor to a creditor, as security for the debt.
To pledge; to make liable to the payment of any debt or expenditure. Already a portion of the entire capital of the nation is mortgaged for the support of drunkards.
mortgaged: mor’gaged. Conveyed in fee as security for the payment of money.
mortgagee: morgagee’. The person to whom an estate is mortgaged.
mortgager: mor’gager. The person who grants an estate as security for a debt, as above specified.
mortiferous: Bringing or producing death; deadly; fatal; destructive.
mortification: In medicine and surgery, the death and consequent putrefaction of one part of any animal body, while the rest is alive; or the loss of heat and action in some part of a living animal, followed by a dissolution of organic texture; gangrene; sphacelus. Mortification is the local or partial death of a living animal body, and if not arrested, soon extinguishes life in the whole body. We usually apply mortification to the local extinction of life and loss or organic texture in a living body. The dissolution of the whole body after death, is called putrefaction.

In Scripture, the act of subduing the passions and appetites by penance, abstinence or painful severities inflicted on the body. The mortification of the body by fasting has been the practice of almost all nations, and the mortification of the appetites and passions by self-denial is always a christian duty: .

Humiliation or slight vexation; the state of being humbled or depressed by disappointment, vexation, crosses, or any thing that wounds or abases pride. It is one of the vexatious mortifications of a studious man to have his thoughts disordered by a tedious visit. We had the mortification to lose sight of Munich, Augsburg and Ratisbon.
Destruction of active qualities; applied to metals.
mortify: To destroy the organic texture and vital functions of some part of a living animal; to change to sphacelus or gangrene. Extreme inflammation speedily mortifies flesh.
To subdue or bring into subjection, as the bodily appetites by abstinence or rigorous severities. We mortify ourselves with fish. With fasting mortified, worn out with tears.
To subdue; to abase; to humble; to reduce; to restrain; as inordinate passions. Mortify thy learned lust. Mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth. Colossians 3:5.

To humble; to depress; to affect with slight vexation. How often is the ambitious man mortified with the very praises he receives, if they do not rise so high as he thinks they ought. He is controlled by a nod, mortified by a frown, and transported with a smile.
To destroy active powers or essential qualities. He mortified pearls in vinegar-- . Quicksilver--mortified with turpentine.
mortifying: Changing from soundness to gangrene or sphacelus.
Subduing; humbling; restraining. 2 .a. Humiliating; tending to humble or abase. He met with a mortifying repulse.
mortise: mor'tis. A cut or hollow place made in timber by the augur and chisel, to receive the tenon of another piece of timber.
To cut or make a mortise in.
To join timbers by a tenon and mortise; as, to mortise a beam into a post, or a joist into a girder.
mortised: Having a mortise; joined by a mortise and tenon.
mortising: Making a mortise; uniting by a mortise and tenon.
mortmain: In law, possession of lands or tenements in dead hands, or hands that cannot alienate. Alienation in mortmain is an alienation of lands or tenements to any corporation, sole or aggregate, ecclesiastical or temporal, particularly to religious houses, by which the estate becomes perpetually inherent in the corporation and unalienable.
mortress: A dish of meat of various kinds beaten together.
mortuary: A sort of ecclesiastical heriot, a customary gift claimed by and due to the minister of a parish on the death of a parishioner. It seems to have been originally a voluntary bequest or donation, intended to make amends for any failure in the payment of tithes of which the deceased had been guilty.
Belonging to the burial of the dead.
mosaic: Mosaic work is an assemblage of little pieces of glass, marble, precious stonesof various colors, cut square and cemented on a ground of stucco, in such a manner as to imitate the colors and gradations of painting.
Pertaining to Moses, the leader of the Israelites; as the Mosaic law, rites or institutions.
moschatel: A plant of the genus Adoxa, hollow root or inglorious. There is one species only, whose leaves and flowers smell like musk; and hence it is sometimes called musk-crowfoot.
mosk: A Mohammedan temple or place of religious worship. Mosks are square building, generally constructed of stone. Before the chief gate is a square court paved with white marble, and surrounded with a low gallery whose roof is supported by pillars of marble. In this gallery the worshipers wash themselves before they enter the mosk.
moss: The mosses are one of the seven families or classes into which all vegetables are divided by Linne in the Philosophia Botanica. In Ray’s method, the mosses form the third class, and in Tournefort’s, they constitute a single genus. In the sexual system, they are the second order of the class cryptogamia, which contains all the plants in which the parts of the flower and fruit are wanting or not conspicuous. The mosses, musci, form a natural order of small plants, with leafy stems and narrow simple leaves. Their flowers are generally monecian or diecian, and their seeds are contained in a capsule covered with a calyptra or hood. The term moss is also applied to many other small plants, particularly lichens, species of which are called tree-moss, rock- moss, coral-mossThe fir-moss and club-moss are of the genus Lycopodium.
A bog; a place where peat is found.
To cover with moss by natural growth. An oak whose boughs were mossed with age.
moss-grown: Overgrown with moss; as moss-grown towers.
mossiness: The state of being overgrown with moss.
mossy: Overgrown with moss; abounding with moss. Old trees are more mossy than young.
Shaded or covered with moss, or bordered with moss; as mossy brooks; mossy fountains.
most: Consisting of the greatest number. That scheme of life is to be preferred, which presents a prospect of the most advantages with the fewest inconveniences. Most men will proclaim every one his own goodness. Proverbs 20:6.
consisting of the greatest quantity; greatest; as the most part of the land or the mountain.

In the greatest or highest degree. Pursue that course of life which will most tend to produce private happiness and public usefulness. Contemplations on the works of God expand the mind and tend to produce most sublime views of his power and wisdom. As most is used to express the superlative degree, it is used before any adjective; as most vile, most wicked, most illustrious.
The greatest number or part.& Then he began to upbraid the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done. Matthew 11:20.
mostic: A painter's staff or stick on which he rests his hand in painting.
mostly: For the greatest part. The exports of the U. States consist mostly of cotton, rice, tobacco, flour and lumber.
motacil: A bird of the genus Motacilla or wagtail.
mote: A small particle; any thing proverbially small; a spot. Why beholdest thou the mote in thy brother’s eye? Vaitthew 7:3. The little motes in the sun do ever stir, though there is no wind.
motet: A musical composition; an air or hymn.
moth: An animal of the genus Phalaena, which breeds in yard and garments, and often does injury by eating the substance and destroying the texture. Matthew 6:19-20. The name is also applied to the whole genus.
Figuratively, that which gradually and silently eats, consumes or wastes any thing. Idle persons are a moth to the community.
motheat: To eat or prey upon, as a moth eats a garment.
mother: [L. mater, mother; matrix, the womb; materia, matter, stuff, materials of which any thing is made. We observe that in some other languages, as well as in English, the same word signifies a female parent, and the thick slime formed in vinegar; and in all the languages of Europe here cited, the orthography is nearly the same as that of mud and matter. The question then occurs whether the name of a female parent originated in a word expressing matter, mold; either the soil of the earth, as the producer, or the like substance, when shaped and fitted as a mold for castings; or whether the name is connected with the opinion that the earth is the mother of all productions; whence the word mother-earth. We are informed by a fragment of Sanchoniathon, that the ancient Phenicians considered mud to be the substance from which all things were formed. See IViud. The word matter is evidently from the

That which has produced any thing. Alas, poor country! it cannot . Be called our mother, but our grave. So our native land is called mother country, and a plant from which a slip or cion is taken, is called the mother plant. In this use, mother may be considered as an adjective.
That which has preceded in time; the oldest or chief of any thing; as a mother-church.
A familiar term of address or appellation of an old woman or matron.
motherhood: The state of being a mother.
mother-in-law: The mother of a husband or wife.
motherless: Destitute of a mother; having lost a mother; as motherless children.
motherly: Pertaining to a mother; as motherly power or authority.
Becoming a mother; tender; parental; as motherly love or care.
In the manner of a mother.
mother of pearl: The matrix of pearl; the shell in which pearls are generated; a species of Mytilus or Mussel.
mother of thyme: A plant of the genus Thymus.
mother-water: A fluid remaining after the evaporation of salt water, and containing deliquescent salts and impurities.
mother-wort: A plant of the genus Leonurus.
mothery: Concreted; resembling or partaking of the nature of mother; as the mothery substance in liquors.
mothy: Full of moths; as an old mothy saddle.
motion: The act or process of changing place; change of local position; the passing of a body from one place to another; change of distance between bodies; opposed to rest. Animal motion is that which is performed by animals in consequence of volition or an act of the will; but how the will operates on the body in producing motion, we cannot explain. Mechanical motion is effected by the force or power of one body acting on another. Perpetual motion is that which is effected or supplied by itself, without the impulse or intervention of any external cause. Hitherto it has been found impossible to invent a machine that has this principle.
Animal life and action. Devoid of sense and motion.
Manner of moving the body; port; gait; air. Each member move and every motion guide.&
Change of posture; action. Watching the motion of her patron’s eye.
motionless: Wanting motion; being at rest. I grow a statue, fixed and motionless.
motive: Causing motion; having power to move or tending to move; as a motive argument; motive power.

That which incites to action; that which determines the choice, or moves the will. Thus we speak of good motives, and bad motives; strong and weak motives. The motive to continue at rest is ease or satisfaction; the motive to change is uneasiness, or the prospect of good.
That which may or ought to incite to action; reason; cause.
motley: Variegated in color; consisting of different colors; dappled; as a motley coat.
Composed of different or various parts, characters or kinds; diversified; as a motley style. And doubts of motley hue.
motor: A mover. The metals are called motors of electricity.
motto: Primarily, a word; but more commonly, a sentence or phrase prefixed to an essay or discourse, containing the subject of it, or added to a device. In heraldry, the motto is carried in a scroll, alluding to the bearing or to the name of the bearer, or expressing some important idea.
mound: Something raised as a defense or fortification, usually a bank of earth or stone; a bulwark; a rampart or fence. God has thrown . That mountain as his garden mound, high raised. To thrid the thickets or to leap the mounds.
mounded: Surrounded or defended by mounds. The lakes high mounded. WOUNDED.
mount: A mass of earth, or earth and rock, rising considerably above the common surface of the surrounding land. Mount is used for an eminence or elevation of earth, indefinite in highth or size, and may be a hillock, hill or mountain. We apply it to Mount Blanc, in Switzerland, to Mount Tom and Mount Holyoke, in Massachusetts, and it is applied in Scripture to the small hillocks on which sacrifice was offered as well as to Mount Sinai. Jacob offered sacrifice on the mount or heap of stones raised for a witness between him and Laban. Genesis 31:54.
Amound; a bulwark for offense or defense. Hew ye down trees and cast a mount against Jerusalem. Jeremiah 6:6.
Formerly, a bank or fund of money.
To rise on high; to ascend; with or without up. Doth the eagle mount up at thy command? Job 39:27. The fire of trees and houses mounts on high.
To rise; to ascend; to tower; to be built to a great altitude. Though Babylon should mount up to heaven. Jeremiah 51:53.
mountain: A large mass of earth and rock, rising above the common level of the earth or adjacent land, but of no definite altitude. We apply mountain to the largest eminences on the globe; but sometimes the word is used for a large hill. In general, mountain denotes an elevation higher and larger than a hill; as the Altaic mountains in Asia, the Alps in Switzerland, the Andes in South America, the Allegheny mountains in Virginia, the Catskill in New York, the White mountains in New Hampshire, and the Green mountains in Vermont. The word is applied to a single elevation, or to an extended range.
Pertaining to a mountain; found on mountains; growing or dwelling on a mountain; as mountain air; mountain pines; mountain goats.
mountaineer: A rustic; a freebooter; a savage.
mountainer: A rustic; a freebooter; a savage.
mountainous: Full of mountains; as the mountainous country of the Swiss.
Large as a mountain; huge; as a mountainous heap.
mountainousness: The state of being full of mountains.
mountain-parsley: A plant of the genus Athamanta.
mountain-soap: A mineral of a pale brownish black color.
mountebank: To cheat by boasting and false pretenses; to gull.

One who mounts a bench or state in the market or other public place, boasts of his skill in curing diseases, vends medicines which he pretends are infallible remedies, and thus deludes the ignorant multitude. Persons of this character may be indicted and punished.
Any boastful and false pretender. Nothing so impossible in nature, but mountebanks will undertake.
mounted: Raised; seated on horseback; placed on a carriage; covered or embellished; furnished with guns.
mounting: Rising; soaring; placing on horseback; ascending an eminence; embellishing.
mourn: To express grief or sorrow; to grieve; to be sorrowful. Mourning may be expressed by weeping or audible sounds, or by sobs, sighs or inward silent grief. Abraham came to mourn for Sarah and to weep. Genesis 23:2. Blessed are they that mourn, for they shall be comforted. atihew 5:4.
To wear the customary habit of sorrow. We mourn in black. Grieve for an hour perhaps, then mourn a year.

To grieve for; to lament. But there is an ellipsis of for, the verb not being transitive. When we say, we mourn a friend or a child, the real sense and complete phrase is, we mourn for a friend, or mourn for the loss of a friend. “He mourn’d his rival’s ill success,” that is, he mourned for his rival’s ill success.
To utter in a sorrowful manner. The love lorn nightingale . Nightly to thee her sad song mourneth well.
mourne: The round end of a staff; the part of a lance to which the steel is fixed, or the ferrel.
mourner: One that mourns or is grieved at any loss or misfortune.
One that follows a funeral in the habit of mourning.
Something used at funerals. The mourner eugh and builder oak were there.
mournful: Intended to express sorrow, or exhibiting the appearance of grief, as a mournful bell; mournful music. No funeral rites nor man in mournful weeds.
Causing sorrow; sad; calamitous; as a mournful death.
Sorrowful; feeling grief. The mournful fair-- . Shall visit her distinguished urn.
mournfully: In a manner expressive of sorrow; with sorrow. Malachi 3:14.
mourning: Grieving; lamenting; sorrowing; wearing the appearance of sorrow.
The act of sorrowing or expressing grief; lamentation; sorrow.
The dress or customary habit worn by mourners. And evn the pavements were with mourning hid.
mourning-dove: A species of dove found in the U. States, the Columba Caroliniensis.
mouse: Asmall animal of the genus Mus, inhabiting houses. The name is also applied to many other species of the genus, as the field mouse, meadow mouse, rock mouse.
Among seamen, a knob formed on a rope by spun yarn or parceling.
mouz. To tear, as a cat devours a mouse. To mouse a hook, with seamen, is to fasten a small line across the upper part to prevent unhooking.
mouse-ear: mouse’-ear. A plant of the genus Hieracium; also, a plant of the genus Myosotis, called likewise mouse-ear scorpion grass. The mouse-ear chickweed is of the genus Cerastium.
mouse-hole: mous’hole. A hole where mice enter or pass; a very small hole or entrance. He can creep in at a mouse-hole.
mouse-hunt: A mouser; one that hunts mice.
mouser: mouz’er. One that catches mice. The cat is a good mouser.
mouse-tail: mous’-tail. A plant of the genus Myosurus.
mouse-trap: mous’trap. A trap for catching mice.
mouth: The aperture in the head of an animal, between the lips, by which he utters his voice and receives food. In a more general sense, the mouth consists of the lips, the gums, the insides of the cheeks, the palate, the salival glands, the uvula and tonsils.
The opening of a vessel by which it is filled or emptied; as the mouth of a jar or pitcher.
The part or channel of a river by which its waters are discharged into the ocean or into a lake. The Mississippi and the Nile discharge their waters by several mouths.
The opening of a piece of ordnance at the end, by which the charge issues.
The aperture of a vessel in animal bodies, by which fluids or other matter is received or discharged; as the mouth of the lacteals.
mouthed: Uttered with a full, swelling, affected voice.

a. Furnished with a mouth; used chiefly in composition; as well- mouthed; foul-mouthed, contumelious, reproachful or obscene; mealy-mouthed, bashful, reserved in speaking the plain truth; hard- mouthed, as a horse, not obedient to the bit, difficult to be restrained or governed by the bridle.
Borne down or overpowered by clamor.
mouthfriend: One who professes friendship without entertaining it; a pretended friend.
mouthful: As much as the mouth contains at once.
A quantity proverbially small; a small quantity.
mouthing: Uttering with an affected swelling voice.
mouthpiece: The piece of a musical wind instrument to which the mouth is applied.
movable: That may be moved; that can or may be lifted, carried, drawn, turned or conveyed, or in any way made to change place or posture; susceptible of motion.
That may or does change from one time to another; as a movable feast. A movable letter, in Hebrew grammar, is one that is pronounced, as opposed to one that is quiescent.
movableness: The state or quality of being movable; mobility; susceptibility of motion.
movables: Goods, wares, commodities, furniture; any species of property not fixed, and thus distinguished from houses and lands.
movably: So that it may be moved.
move: To impel; to carry, convey or draw from one place to another; to cause to change place or posture in any manner or by any means. The wind moves a ship; the cartman moves goods; the horse moves a cart or carriage. Mere matter cannot move itself. Machines are moved by springs, weights, or force applied.
To excite into action; to affect; to agitate; to rouse; as, to move the passions.
To cause to act or determine; as, to move the will.

To persuade; to prevail on; to excite from a state of rest or indifference. Minds desirous of revenge were not moved with gold. But when no female arts his mind could move, She turn’d to furious hate her impious love.
moveless: That cannot be moved; fixed. The Grecian phalanx, moveless as a tower.
movement: Motion; a passing, progression, shaking, turning or flowing; any change of position in a material body; as the movement of an army in marching or maneuvering; the movement of a wheel or a machine.
Excitement; agitation; as the movement of the mind.
In music, any single strain or part having the same measure or time. Any change of time is a change of movement.
mover: The person or thing that gives motion or impels to action.
A proposer; one that offers a proposition, or recommends any thing for consideration or adoption; as the mover of a resolution in a legislative body.
moving: Causing to move or act; impelling; instigating; persuading; influencing. 1 .a. Exciting the passions or affections; touching; pathetic; affecting; adapted to excite or affect the passions; as a moving address or discourse.
movingly: In a manner to excite the passions or affect sensibility; pathetically. His air, his voice, his looks and honest soul, WOVINGLY. Speak all so movingly in his behalf.
movingness: The power of affecting, as the passions.
mow: A heap, mass or pile of hay deposited in a barn.
To lay hay in a heap or mass in a barn, or to lay it in a suitable manner.
pret. mower; pp. mowed or mown.
To cut down with a scythe, as grass or other plants. We say, to mow grass.
To cut the grass from; as, to mow a meadow.
mowburn: To heat and ferment in the mow, as hay when housed too green.
mowed: Cleared of grass with a scythe, as land.
mown: Cleared of grass with a scythe, as land.
mower: One who mows; a man dexterous in the use of the scythe.
mowing: The act of cutting with a scythe.
Land from which grass is cut.
moxa: The down of the mugwort of China; a soft lanuginous substance prepared in Japan from the young leaves of a species of Artemisia. In the eastern countries, it is used for the goutby burning it on the skin. This produces a dark colored spot, the exulceration of which is promoted by applying a little garlic.
much: Great in quantity or amount. Thou shalt carry much seed into the field, and gather but little in. Deuteronomy 28:38. Manasseh wrought much wickedness in the sight of the Lord to provoke him to anger. 2 Kings 21:6. Return with much riches to your tents. Joshua 22:8.
Long in duration. How much time is spent in trifling amusements!
Many in number. Edom came out against him with much people. Numbers 20:20.

In a great degree; by far; qualifying adjectives of the comparative degree; as much more, much stronger, much heavier, much more splendid, much higher. So we say, much less, much smaller, much less distinguished, much weaker, much finer.

To a great degree or extent; qualifying verbs and participles. Jonathan, Saul’s son, delighted much in David.7 Samuel 19:2. It is a night to be much observed. Exodus 12:42. The soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way. Numbers 21:4. A much afflicted, much enduring man.
mucic: The mucic acid is the same as the saccholactic. It is obtained from gums
mucilage: In chimistry, one of the proximate elements of vegetables. The same substance is a gum when solid, and a mucilage when in solution. Both the ingredients improve one another; for the mucilage adds to the lubricity of the oil, and the oil preserves the mucilage from inspissation. Mucilage is obtained from vegetable or animal substances.
The liquor which moistens and lubricates the ligaments and cartilages of the articulations or joints in animal bodies.
mucilaginous: Pertaining to or secreting mucilage; as the mucilaginous glands.
Slimy; ropy; moist, soft and lubricous; partaking of the nature of mucilage; as a mucilaginous gum.
mucilaginousness: Sliminess; the state of being mucilaginous.
mucite: A combination of a substance with mucous acid.
muck: Dung in a moist state, or a mass of dung and putrefied vegetable matter. With fattening muck besmear the roots.

Something mean, vile or filthy. To run a muck, to run madly and attack all we meet. Running a muck, is a phrase derived from the Malays, (in whose language amock signified to kill,) applied to desperate persons who intoxicate themselves with opium and then arm themselves with a dagger and attempt to kill all they meet.
mucker: To scrape together money by mean labor or shifts.
muckworm: A worm that lives in muck.
A miser; one who scrapes together money by mean labor and devices.
mucoso-saccharine: Partaking of the qualities of mucilage and sugar.
mucous: Pertaining to mucus or resembling it; slimy, ropy and lubricous; as a mucous substance.
Secreting a slimy substance; as the mucous membrane. The mucous membrane lines all the cavities of the body which open externally, and secretes the fluid called mucus.
mucousness: The state of being mucous; sliminess.
mucronate: Narrowed to a point; terminating in a point.
mucronated: Narrowed to a point; terminating in a point.
mucus: A viscid fluid secreted by the mucous membrane, which it serves to moisten and defend. It covers the lining membranes of all the cavities which open externally, such as those of the mouth, nose, lungs, intestinal canal, urinary passagesIt differs from gelatine. In the action of chewing, the mucus mixeth with the ailment.
This term has also been applied to other animal fluids of a viscid quality, as the synovial fluid, which lubricates the cavities of the joints.
mud: Moist and soft earth of any kind, such as is found in marshes and swamps, at the bottom or rivers and ponds, or in highways after rain.
To bury in mud or slime.
To make turbid or foul with dirt; to stir the sediment in liquors.
muddily: Turbidly; with foul mixture. Lucilius--writ loosely and muddily.
muddiness: Turbidness; foulness caused by mud, dirt or sediment; as the muddiness of a stream.
muddle: To make foul, turbid or muddy, as water. He did ill to muddle the water.
To intoxicate partially; to cloud or stupefy, particularly with liquor. He was often drunk, always muddled. Epicurus seems to have had his brains muddled.
muddling: Making foul with dirt or dregs; making half drunk; stupefying.
muddy: Foul with dirt or fine earthy particles; turbid, as water or other fluids; as a muddy stream. Water running on fine clay always appears muddy.
Containing mud; as a muddy ditch; a muddy road.
Dirty; dashed, soiled or besmeared with mud; as muddy boots.
Consisting of mud or earth; gross, impure; as this muddy vesture of decay.
Dark; of the color of mud; as muddy cheeks.
mud-fish: A fish, a species of the cyprinus kind.
mud-sill: In bridges, the sill that is laid at the bottom of a river, lake
mud-wall: A wall composed of mud, or of materials laid in mud without mortar.
mudwort: A species of Limosella, the least water plaintain.
muff: A warm cover for the hands, usually made of fur or dressed skins.
muffle: To cover from the weather by cloth, fur or any garment; to cover close, particularly the neck and face. You must be muffled up like ladies. The face lies muffled up within the garment.
To blindfold. Alas! that love whose view if muffled still-- WUFFLE. He muffled with a cloud his mournful eyes.
To cover; to conceal; to involve. They were in former ages muffled in darkness and superstition.
In seamanship, to put matting or other soft substance round an oar, to prevent its making a noise.
To wind something round the strings of a drum to prevent a sharp sound, or to render the sound grave and solemn.
muffled: Covered closely, especially about the face; involved; blindfolded.
muffler: A cover for the face; a part of female dress.
muffling: Covering closely, especially about the face; wrapping close; involving; blindfolding.
mufti: The high priest or chief of the ecclesiastical order among the Mohammedans.
mug: A kind of cup from which liquors are drank. In American, the word is applied chiefly or solely to an earthen cup.
muggnet: A species of wild fresh water duck.
muggish: Moist; damp;; moldy; as muggy straw.
Moist; damp; close; warm and unelastic; as muggy air.
muggy: Moist; damp;; moldy; as muggy straw.
Moist; damp; close; warm and unelastic; as muggy air.
mugil: The mullet, a genus of fishes of the order of abdominals.
mugweed: A plant of the genus Valantia.
mugwort: A plant of the genus Artemisia.
mulatto: A person that is the offspring of a negress by a white man, or of a white woman by a negro.
mulberry: The berry or fruit of a true of the genus Morus.
mulberry-tree: The tree which produces the mulberry.
mulct: A fine imposed on a person guilty of some offense or misdemeanor, usually a pecuniary fine.
To fine; to punish for an offense or misdemeanor by imposing a pecuniary fine.
mule: A quadruped of a mongrel breed, usually generated between an ass and a mare, sometimes between a horse and a she-ass. But the name is applied to any animal produced by a mixture of different species.
A plant or vegetable produced by impregnating the pistil of one species of plant with the farin or fecundating dust of another. This is called also a hybrid.
mule-wort: A plant of the genus Hemionitis.
muliebrity: Womanhood; the state of being a woman; a state in females corresponding to virility in man; also, effeminacy; softness.
mulier: In law, lawful issue born in wedlock though begotten before.
mull: To soften; or to heat, sweeten and enrich with spices; as, to mull wine. Drink new cider, mull’d with ginger warm.
In Scottish, a snuff-box, made of the small end of a horn.
mullen: A plant of the genus Verbascum.
muller: A stone held in the hand with which colors and other matters are ground on another stone; used by painters and apothecaries.
An instrument used by glass grinders, being a piece of wood with the piece of glass to be ground cemented to one end, either convex in a basin, or concave in a sphere or bowl.
mullet: A fish of the genus Mugil. The lips are membranaceous; the inferior one carinated inwards; it has no teeth, and the body is of a whitish color. This fish frequents the shore and roots in the sand like a hog. It is an excellent fish for the table.
mulligrubs: A twisting of the intestines; sullenness.
mullion: A division in a window frame; a bar.
mulse: Wine boiled and mingled with honey.
multifarious: Having great multiplicity; having great diversity or variety; as multifarious artifice.
multifariously: With great multiplicity and diversity; with great variety of modes and relations.
multiform: Having many forms, shapes or appearances; as the multiform operations of the air-pump.
multiformity: Diversity of forms; variety of shapes or appearances in the same thing.
multilateral: many sides. A multilateral figure must also be multangular.
multilocular: Having many cells; as a multilocular pericarp.
multiparous: Producing many at a birth. A serpent is a multiparous animal.
multipartite: Divided into many parts; having’ several parts.
multiped: An insect that has many feet.
multiple: In arithmetic, a common multiple of two or more numbers contains each of them a certain number of times exactly; thus 24 is a common multiple of 3 and 4. But the least common multiple, is the least number which will do this; thus 12 is the least common multiple of 3 and 4.
multiplex: Many-fold; having petals lying over each other in folds.
multiplicand: In arithmetic, the number to be multiplied by another, which is called the multiplier.
multiplicate: Consisting of many, or more than one.
A multiplicate flower is a sort of luxuriant flower, having the corol multiplied so far as to exclude only some of the stamens.
multiplication: The act of multiplying or of increasing number; as the multiplication of the human species by natural generation.
In arithmetic, a rule or operation by which any given number may be increased according to any number of times proposed. Thus multiplied by 5 is increased to 50.
multiplicative: Tending to multiply; having the power to multiply or increase numbers.
multiplicator: The number by which another number is multiplied; a multiplier.
multiplicity: A state of being many; as a multiplicity of thoughts or objects.
Many of the same kind. The pagans of antiquity had a multiplicity of deities.
multiplied: Numerous; often repeated; as multiplied aggressions.
multiplier: One who multiplies, or increases number.
The number in arithmetic by which another is multiplied; the multiplicator.
